0: One of the chapters, Chapter 2 was published as a manuscript in the Agronomy Journal. Reproduced with permission from John Wiley and Sons (Appendix A). Chapters 1, 3-7 - Copyright © 2023 Heidi Sieverding
1: Climate-smart agricultural products are increasing the need for farmer-focused life cycle analyses (LCA) for decision-making. The environmental benefits and impacts of their production practices needs to be effectively communicated to agricultural producers using terms that they understand and that are important to them. Soil carbon is an important component of soil health and fertility. Soil carbon is an environmental impact indicator that many farmers know and understand and it has the potential to offset greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, yet, it is not included in many biofuel GHG accounting programs. Amending soil with manure and compost is a well-known and documented regenerative practice, however, the permanence of soil carbon changes due to organic amendments is questioned by the scientific community. The objective of this dissertation is to develop technical guidance documentation based on meta-analytical results and sensitivity analyses that can be used by farmers and policy-makers in decision-making. This research follows ISO 14044 life cycle assessment (LCA) methods and meta- analytical methods following Xu et al., 2019. Cradle to field gate analyses are frequently used to evaluate and compare crop production practices. Amending soils with manure or compost on average increased soil organic carbon (SOC) within soils by 0.4 Mg/ha-yr. Use of regenerative practices such as manure amendment continued to incrementally increase SOC even after 20 years of application, supporting that SOC levels can reach a new, dynamic equilibrium through use of manure amendments. Carinata (Brassica carinata) grown with a low level of field inputs (<56 kg N/ha) can have yields as low as 1000-2000 kg/ha and still meet renewable fuel standards. Carinata produced with greater field inputs (100-120 kg N/ha) required greater yields (3000-4500 kg/ha) to meet renewable fuel standards. Regenerative management practices support climate-smart agricultural production by reducing the need for field inputs and the yields necessary to meet emission targets. Three publications were developed: 1) an agriculturally-focused LCA primer, 2) a global meta-analysis of the long-term soil carbon impacts of manure and compost amendments, and 3) a LCA sensitivity analysis that provides yield-targets for climate-smart crop production.
2: To my family, advisors, collaborators, committee, and department - thank you for supporting me in completing my degree. I would not have been able to complete it without your support.
This research was funded by the South Dakota Corn Utilization Council and South Dakota Oilseeds Initiative.

16: Cropland production systems are essential to food, feed, forage, fiber, and fuel production. The Green Revolution enabled global populations to increase dramatically but a population tipping point is forecasted to occur within this century unless significant changes to consumption or technological advances are made (Bystroff 2021, UN 2022). During November 2022, the United Nations (UN) announced that the global human population of the world surpassed 8 billion people (Nations' 2022). Recent global Landsat satellite data analyses indicate the global average per capita cropland area land use is 0.16 ha per person and overall cropland land use (1,244 Mha in 2019) is increasing with human population, largely at the cost of natural forests and grasslands in Africa and South America(Potapov, Turubanova et al. 2021). Combining UN population estimates with remote sensing analyses by Potapov et al. 2022, approximately 9% of the Earth's land surface is likely currently being used for crop production. This increase cannot continue indefinitely because the quantity of arable land is limited (~10% of global land area), and agricultural production is dependent on already stressed ecosystems (Pravalie, Patriche et al. 2021). Regenerative agricultural production, a method of building and restoring soil health, and climate-smart practices are being widely implemented by the United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) to lessen the impact of crop production and restore degraded lands (USDA 2021). Crop production affects ecosystems and natural cycles at multiple scales, from microscopic to global. A systemic, holistic approach such as life cycle analysis (LCA) is needed to evaluate the complex trade-offs associated with agricultural production practices and to identify improvement opportunities and quantify benefits associated with changed practices.

Agricultural products are grown to be consumed. Their natural fate is to continue the carbon cycle, regardless of how long the storage duration (moments to millennia) may be. People interrupt the natural cycle by creating more durable goods such as bioplastics or accelerate it by burning fossil fuels or eroding soils. Land-based agricultural systems use and manipulate natural biological processes and land resources to serve humanity. Their existence unavoidably comes at some cost to native ecosystems. It has increasingly been recognized by society that many agricultural production practices are unsustainable. Yet simultaneously, advanced bioproducts are increasingly looked towards as a panacea for fossil fuel use and derived products, such as conventional plastics. For many biofuels and bioproducts, a significant portion of the environmental burden is acquired during agricultural production. Understanding and systematically evaluating agricultural production practices is critical to establishing sustainable pathways for future generations. This dissertation will investigate 1) LCA practices and how they are used to evaluate the sustainability of agricultural products, 2) soil carbon changes associated with a common regenerative practice of amending soil with compost or manure, and 3) how agricultural production practices and inclusion of soil carbon affects LCA impacts and the yields necessary for farmers to meet sustainability standards.

18: Manure soil amendments to croplands positively affects life cycle greenhouse gas footprint through soil carbon sequestration; therefore, soil carbon impacts should be included in biofuel and bioproduct greenhouse gas accounting.

20: Sustainable crop production is essential to the fate and future of humanity and ecosystems. The food, feed, forage, and fiber that humanity needs simply cannot be produced without consequences. As a result, systemically managing benefits and costs requires constant consideration and effort to balance trade-offs. Developing resilient agricultural practices and sustainable food production systems is a critical issue that crosscuts most of the United Nations' sustainable development goals (Nations 2022). The use of LCA in evaluating product sustainability is commonplace within the European Union (EU) and is rapidly increasing in the United States (US). US Presidential Executive Order 14057 implements several sustainability measures for the federal government, including purchasing policies that phase in net zero GHG footprint and environmental evaluation for federally purchased products. This sustainable purchasing requirement is broad and includes food, fiber, and fuel purchases. The EPA has begun implementing this policy and is encouraging use of holistic LCA methods, largely following LCA practices established through the biofuel Renewable Identification Number (RIN) and European Union's Renewable Energy Directive (RED) programs, to
evaluate ecolabeling of products. It is anticipated that ecolabels will be embraced by consumers and will be continued regardless of federal policy due to consumer demand.

22: <header>1.4. Background </header>

This dissertation is focused on LCA of agricultural products. A comprehensive understanding of environmental accounting practices and methods is needed. The life cycle evaluation process is iterative. The evaluations conducted as part of this paper will predominantly be cradle-to-field-gate, however, those using biofuel policies as the end point will require reversing the analysis to identify agricultural practices and associated yields needed to meet emission and footprint standards.

Agricultural production systems terminology and practices vary regionally. An understanding of agricultural systems and crop-specific practices is needed to develop appropriate inventories and life cycle evaluations. To evaluate the systemic effect across climates and geographies of manure and compost amendment on soil carbon, a global meta-analysis needs to be undertaken. This research will require an understanding not only of LCA but also of agricultural practices, agricultural terminology, crop rotations, agricultural experimental design, statistics, soil pedogenesis, and biogeochemical cycling. This evaluation will focus on biofuel feedstocks of carinata (Brassica carinata) and corn/maize (Zea mays). Carinata is an emerging industrial oilseed and advanced biofuel and bioproduct feedstock. Corn is a globally established commodity that is produced worldwide and has been extensively studied by the scientific community, enabling a global meta-analysis to be conducted. New feedstock crops such as carinata, face many roadblocks when being introduced. Farmers do not want to produce a new crop without a

guaranteed buyer but bio-processors do not want to use a new feedstock unless they have a guaranteed supply. Neither farmer nor bio-processor can afford the investment and risk associated with upscaling production of new feedstocks. As a result, introduction of new crops or feedstocks often reaches an impasse. Life cycle analyses must be conducted to document the sustainability of new feedstocks or processes, prior to them reaching a commercial scale. As a result, assumptions must be for new crops or bioprocesses based on others with more robust documentation. This dissertation will explore the use of data from a well-documented crop, corn on management practice and soil carbon sequestration to make modeling assumptions for an emerging crop, carinata.

Regenerative agricultural production systems strive to mimic natural systems and create resilient agroecosystems which benefit both people and nature. Regenerative systems often focus on improving soil health and increasing soil carbon due to the critical role soil has in sustaining crop production. Soil health is complex and can be costly to evaluate, whereas soil organic carbon is comparatively cheap and easy to measure -and- it is a variable affecting greenhouse gas (GHG) accounting.

Greenhouse gas accounting is one of the most widely recognized and used life cycle impact assessments (LCIA). The role of soil carbon in GHG accounting has been widely debated. Many scientists question the permanence of soil carbon and as a result, it is not included federal [Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)] or state [California Air Resources Board (CARB)] biofuel GHG accounting policies. Additionally, an average feedstock GHG footprint is used in GHG biofuel accounting at the time of pathway approval (Faaij 2022), not feedstock specific accounting that is being targeted by the USDA's new climate-smart commodities program (USDA 2021). An understanding of

policies regulating and mandating product sustainability is needed to evaluate how including soil organic carbon will affect results. Additionally, an understanding of other environmental impacts and how they may interact with changing ecolabeling practices (EO 14057) will be needed. The EPA is currently developing ecolabeling standards to meet EO 14057. These ecolabels are GHG focused, however, the EPA is encouraging a life cycle approach which also considers hotspot analysis (EPA 2023). This will require a comprehensive understanding of other environmental impacts such as eutrophication, ecotoxicity, and other metrics and their equivalencies.

As the climate changes, it is likely that organic material accumulated over millennia in soils will be lost due to acceleration of biogeochemical processes. The science and policy surrounding climate-smart commodity production must be dynamic and iterative. Policymakers and agricultural producers need to quickly learn and understand complex system feedbacks and analyses that many professionals in the scientific and engineering community struggle to understand. This will not be possible without communicating results and impacts in terms farmers and policymakers understand, such as yield, and development of highly adaptable modeling systems. Model assumptions must be made, tested, then improved repeatedly and rapidly to address changing systems and adoption of transformative practices and technologies. Society at all levels needs to recognize and understand environmental accounting practices. The development and implementation of ecolabels will likely increase demand for LCA-based resources and tools to help individuals in decision-making.

These ecolabels are GHG focused, however, the EPA is encouraging a life cycle approach which also considers hotspot analysis (EPA 2023). This will require a comprehensive understanding of other environmental impacts such as eutrophication, ecotoxicity, and other metrics and their equivalencies.

As the climate changes, it is likely that organic material accumulated over millennia in soils will be lost due to acceleration of biogeochemical processes. The science and policy surrounding climate-smart commodity production must be dynamic and iterative. Policymakers and agricultural producers need to quickly learn and understand complex system feedbacks and analyses that many professionals in the scientific and engineering community struggle to understand. This will not be possible without communicating results and impacts in terms farmers and policymakers understand, such as yield, and development of highly adaptable modeling systems. Model assumptions must be made, tested, then improved repeatedly and rapidly to address changing systems and adoption of transformative practices and technologies. Society at all levels needs to recognize and understand environmental accounting practices. The development and implementation of ecolabels will likely increase demand for LCA-based resources and tools to help individuals in decision-making.
27: <header>Chapter 2. A Life Cycle Analysis (LCA) Primer for the Agricultural Community </header>

When consumer purchase products they are increasingly asking if products are sustainably produced and what the effect of the product on global warming is. An abundance of information on product sustainability exists. Unfortunately, integrating sustainability information into production goals and discerning if a product meets these goals is complex.

Harmonized environmental accounting systems have been developed to untangle and evaluate sustainability-related facts and figures. Common techniques to assess sustainability are life cycle analysis (LCA), energy footprint, carbon footprint, water footprint, criteria air pollutants, and environmental risk assessments. Life cycle analysis is a systematic summation of the multiple environmental impacts associated with the production, use, and disposal of a product. The primary difference between a footprint and an LCA is that footprints assess only one emission or resource while LCA integrates more categories, such as land use, water use, and acidification into calculations. For example, carbon footprints focus on greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and as a result it could be considered as a subset of an LCA. In such calculations, each GHG is converted to carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2e) based on the gas atmospheric residence time and radiative forcing. In agriculture, the primary GHGs emitted during crop and livestock production are carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20).

Approximately 23% of total GHG emissions over the last decade have been attributed to agriculture, forestry and other land use activities (IPCC 2019). A large portion of these GHG emissions are associated with denitrification of inorganic nitrogen fertilizer used in crop production, CH4 emitted from livestock production, and loss of CO2 from

deforestation and land use change. Nitrous oxide has 265-298 times the greenhouse gas impact of CO2 whereas CH4 has 21-36 times the impact (Myhre, Shindell et al. 2013). The atmospheric GHG concentration effects residence time and radiative forcing, as a result, the CO2e conversion factors change and are periodically updated.

Life cycle analysis is used to quantify the environmental performance of products, processes, or services, and is increasingly being used as a basis to inform purchasers along the supply chain, including the final consumers (Fava, Baer et al. 2011). Modern decision making and planning relies on numerical analyses. Individuals, communities, governmental agencies, policymakers and industry want to know and understand where, when, why, and how much decisions and investments affect resources and well-being now and into the future. Analyses, such as LCA, provide numeric summaries which can be used in decision-making, planning, policy development, and investing.

The intent of this primer is to provide a resource to people within the agronomic community who are not well-versed in LCA or modeling in general but need to use LCA results. Product labeling of carbon intensities or indexes (CI) is becoming commonplace for biofuels and premium food products. For example, the California Air Resource Board has regulations that require fuels to meet specific emission standards at specific points in time. In this example, the biofuel industry quantifies the CI based on the amount of GHGs emitted per unit of energy produced (e.g. the unit CO2e per megajoule (MJ) of corn ethanol) to meet GHG emission reduction targets. Both the emission standards and CI are calculated using LCA. The widespread adoption of LCAs by industry and governments has led to these analyses affecting our day-to-day lives and decision-making processes. Life cycle analyses are scientific models which can

incorporate a vast and difficult to navigate array of principles, terminology, and components. It can be difficult for people new to life cycle research to identify what within an LCA is important or useful to them. In agriculture, critical components are GHG emissions, land-use change, ecosystem services, air quality, water quantity and quality, and how management impacts these considerations.

Life cycle analyses are a tool to compare products and systems based on the potential environmental impacts with the goal of improving efficiency, reducing waste, and avoiding products and activities that increase the risk of harm to people, society, and the ecosystem (Figure 2.1). These include, but are not limited to, how tillage effects CIs, or how the 4R (Right place, Right source, Right product, and Right timing) effect nitrogen losses. Life cycle methodologies can and are applied to a vast array of human endeavors. The terminology and exact process may vary among LCA but the common thread amongst them is that these tools are used to better understand resource use, identify weaknesses and strengths, and utilize scientific observations to improve production and use of products and services. Within LCAs, agriculture is often considered to be a raw material source. As a result, the efficiency and sustainability of agricultural production affects all downstream products and processes.
Figure 2.1. Conceptual diagram showing factors considered in a life cycle assessment (LCA).

34: <header>2.1. Why do we need agricultural LCAs? </header>

People generally consider agricultural products to be a renewable resource. But they consume limited resources (e.g., water, nutrients, soil) and are typically produced on a finite amount of arable land which is shrinking due to urbanization, erosion, rising sea levels, and salinization. Approximately three quarters of the Earth's ice-free land surface is being used by people for agriculturally related activities as cropland (12%), pastureland (37%), or managed forests (22%) (IPCC 2019). The risk of land degradation due to poor resource management is high. Scientists disagree as to how much land is degraded due to definition and methodological differences; reports classify anywhere from 7 to 45% of the Earth's ice-free land surface as degraded (Gibbs and Salmon 2015). However, scientists agree that the rate of land degradation is exceeding rate of restoration, as a

result, the quantity of highly productive land available for agricultural production will continue to decrease unless action is taken to support restoration (Stavi and Lal 2015). Meanwhile, global populations continue to rise and become more affluent - causing competition for resources, instigating conflict, and relegating 'best use' of resources as a matter of perspective. To a struggling upland farmer, converting grasslands or forests to cropland is a mechanism to preserve their livelihood and feed their families. Whereas, to a lowland urban resident, this same land use change endangers ecosystem services necessary to their survival and livelihoods.

The supply, demand, and consumption of agricultural products and the economic dynamics and trade-offs associated with agricultural production systems has direct and indirect effects on global systemic health, well-being, and land use. Agricultural products (e.g., food, feed, fiber and fuel) often have a shorter life span (weeks to years) than durable products made of steel or wood, which can last decades or even centuries. Many agricultural products also cannot be as easily recycled or reused due to their relatively limited shelf life. Waste and spoilage are common throughout the agricultural product supply chain and have been estimated to be as high as one third of production (Gustavsson, Cederberg et al. 2011) or half of production if overconsumption in wealthier countries is considered as a form of waste (Alexander, Brown et al. 2017). As a result of a multitude of factors, farm activities and agricultural production practices significantly affect the life cycle of countless products and the shape of our civilizations.

37: Farming and ranching require systems-based thinking. A successful agricultural producer must be aware of what is happening in the field and be able to reflect on what occurred over the last few years, as well as plan for future cropping seasons. To appropriately evaluate farming systems or products, LCAs must account for these components as well. Agricultural LCA inputs and results, like farming, are often dictated by natural variations in soil, water, and climatic resources. Most farmers and ranchers use some form of adaptive management to optimize resource use and accommodate production challenges as they arise. Representing these adaptations and their resulting heterogeneity in LCA models is challenging due to the overwhelming number of variables and system interactions. To make the models more manageable, many agricultural LCAs focus on a single crop, component, impact, or region; as a result, these analyses may not reflect all agroecosystem benefits and consequences of production.

Model simulations and analyses of natural systems cannot be expected to be perfect, what they need to be is a benchmark for future comparisons. A well-built LCA model can be used to answer questions, identify knowledge gaps, evaluate efficiency, document improvements, and/or compare alternatives. Good examples of a knowledge gaps might include, how does synchronizing the N supply with plant demand influence N losses or how does vertically integrating the crop and livestock production systems influence production efficiency and emissions? Farm products are commonly used by multiple industries and sectors (Figure 2.2). For example, corn (Zea mays) can be used to produce ethanol or provide feed for livestock. The crop being evaluated may be the same, but the models developed may have differing scope, priorities, evaluation metrics,

products, feedback processes, and standards. A GHG-focused LCA of corn stover-based ethanol production would likely not be appropriate to evaluate the water footprint of snack-sized bags of corn tortilla chips. Because agricultural products are used by SO many industries, care must be taken when using model results to evaluate whether they are representative of the system(s) and product(s) you want to analyze and if they are useful in answering your questions or not.

41: Life cycle analyses are used as both regulatory and marketing tools. Caution must be used when evaluating the usefulness and applicability of LCA inputs, methods, and results. Analyses may be conducted with fundamentally different goals which influences their usefulness and applicability. Intentional or unintentional oversights can

significantly alter findings. For example, a new analyst is asked to develop a quick LCA model to evaluate the GHG footprint of one cornbread muffin for a large baked goods company. In their evaluation, they include transport from the manufacturer in Tennessee where the muffin was baked to distribution center to store and finally to the home of someone in Oregon where it is consumed. A truck delivering a shipment within the supply chain carries more than just one muffin, but in their haste, the analyst did not account for that difference. The resulting model, in this instance, would unintentionally overestimate the GHG footprint. The complexity of LCAs, like any other data-intensive model, can be used to skew and manipulate analyses to produce desired results and mask analytical shortcomings. Which is why the peer-review process is used by many regulators, companies, and academia. Well-constructed analyses are representative, reproducible, transparent, and clearly convey analytical goals, scope, methods, and funding sources.

Most modern, well-constructed LCAs will account for major direct emissions and impacts (Figure 2.3) (Nemecek and Schnetzer 2012). However, not all LCAs account for indirect emissions and impacts. Indirect impacts vary based on the methodology and factors used to calculate them. No scientific consensus as to how they should be calculated exists due to their complexity, however, researchers agree that indirect impacts can have a substantial global effect (Chen, Qin et al. 2018). For example, it is well recognized that altering corn grain use from feed to biofuel can indirectly influence global land use changes and associated impacts to food production (Figure 2.4). You may ask, what does US ethanol have to do with the price of rice? As a globally important commodity with multiple uses, domestic and international production, use, and

distribution of corn can have a cascading effect on many other food and bioproduct markets. As an extreme example, if all farmers in Nebraska, US or Cordoba, Argentina stopped producing corn, this reduction would have global market consequences and a cascading effect. Market demand would exceed supply, causing corn prices to rise. As a result, farmers elsewhere will likely increase corn production at the cost of other major crop types, particularly rice, or by converting natural areas to crop production. The change in crop selection might increase the price of rice because of a reduction in rice supply. Indirect impacts, particularly with respect to land use, are often recognized and included in governmental policies and life cycle accounting. Indirect impacts affect the relative contribution of sources and sinks within LCAs and are important to note when using and interpreting LCAs.

Figure 2.3. Sankey diagram of US average corn-grain ethanol direct GHG contributions (without land use change or indirect effects) based on data from ICF International, Inc. 2014 survey and consequential LCA scenario results (Flugge, Lewandrowski et al. 2017). In this visualization, livestock feed co-product credits are applied to farm-related emissions to represent net farm-related impacts.
<title>Corn-Grain Ethanol US National Average Indirect & Direct Greenhouse Gas Emissions Using Data from ICF (2014) </title>
Feedstock and fuel transport, fuel production, and tailpipe or grave
Direct and Indirect Land Use Change (LUC)

Figure 2.4. Sankey diagram of US average corn-grain ethanol GHG contributions with indirect effects based on ICF 2014 survey and consequential LCA scenario results (Flugge, Lewandrowski et al. 2017). In this visualization, livestock feed co-product credits are applied to farm-related emissions to represent net farm-related impacts and domestic and international livestock, rice methane, and land use change indirect impacts were assumed to be additive.

It is likely that in the future, more indirect effects of agriculture on ecosystems services and biodiversity will be recognized. For example, salt cedar (Tamarix sp.) was imported in the early 1900s for use as wind breaks to reduce soil erosion on US croplands but at a substantial cost to native plants, water resources, and soil salinity. Deliberate introductions of non-native plants, insects, and animals has created negative agroecosystem impacts and costly, ongoing remediation efforts. In another example, pesticides, such as neonicotinoid-based insecticides, are used in the US to control insect infestations and protect plants and seeds from damage which can minimize crop yield losses due to insects and pathogens. However, the unintentional effect insecticide use has on beneficial insects, pollinators, and agroecosystems is a growing concern. The United Nations (UN) sponsored a global scientific study on human impacts to biodiversity and ecosystem services [Intergovernmental science-policy Platform on Biodiversity and

Ecosystem Services (IPBES)]. The IPBES panel has identified key indirect effects due to invasive species, pollution, and other challenges to nature on global ecosystems and food security, some of which, will likely be addressed by future policy changes.

Over the last two decades, there have been significant advances in the analytical tools, inventories, and resources available to conduct LCAs (UN Environment 2019). The use of LCA within biofuel-related policies, rules, and regulations has increased due to growing food and fuel sustainability challenges (Bhat 2017, Sonnemann, Gemechu et al. 2018). The use of LCA extends beyond the energy sector, some nations, such as the European Union (EU), legislatively support [(COM 2003)302] use of LCA as a sustainable development strategy across sectors. With these changes, the number, types, and complexity of LCAs have proliferated and diversified. Concurrently, there have been significant changes in agricultural production (e.g. precision agriculture, no-till, agrochemicals and biologicals), biorefining technologies (e.g. milling techniques, co- products, heat exchangers, water recycling) and distribution (e.g. supply chains, storage, safety, traceability, transport efficiency). As a result, the temporal and spatial relevancy of inventories and methodologies need to be considered when evaluating and using past work. For example, a national GHG footprint of the US dry-mill ethanol industry from the 1980s would not be representative of a newly constructed, 450 million liter (120 million gallon) per year wet-mill ethanol biorefinery in Iowa because of changes in milling processes. However, this older evaluation could provide a valuable resource to document improvements. When used appropriately LCAs can be a tool to improve efficiency and sustainability of production (Notarnicola, Salomone et al. 2015, Poore and Nemecek 2018).

It is likely that in the future, more indirect effects of agriculture on ecosystems services and biodiversity will be recognized. For example, salt cedar (Tamarix sp.) was imported in the early 1900s for use as wind breaks to reduce soil erosion on US croplands but at a substantial cost to native plants, water resources, and soil salinity. Deliberate introductions of non-native plants, insects, and animals has created negative agroecosystem impacts and costly, ongoing remediation efforts. In another example, pesticides, such as neonicotinoid-based insecticides, are used in the US to control insect infestations and protect plants and seeds from damage which can minimize crop yield losses due to insects and pathogens. However, the unintentional effect insecticide use has on beneficial insects, pollinators, and agroecosystems is a growing concern. The United Nations (UN) sponsored a global scientific study on human impacts to biodiversity and ecosystem services [Intergovernmental science-policy Platform on Biodiversity and

Ecosystem Services (IPBES)]. The IPBES panel has identified key indirect effects due to invasive species, pollution, and other challenges to nature on global ecosystems and food security, some of which, will likely be addressed by future policy changes.

Over the last two decades, there have been significant advances in the analytical tools, inventories, and resources available to conduct LCAs (UN Environment 2019). The use of LCA within biofuel-related policies, rules, and regulations has increased due to growing food and fuel sustainability challenges (Bhat 2017, Sonnemann, Gemechu et al. 2018). The use of LCA extends beyond the energy sector, some nations, such as the European Union (EU), legislatively support [(COM 2003)302] use of LCA as a sustainable development strategy across sectors. With these changes, the number, types, and complexity of LCAs have proliferated and diversified. Concurrently, there have been significant changes in agricultural production (e.g. precision agriculture, no-till, agrochemicals and biologicals), biorefining technologies (e.g. milling techniques, co- products, heat exchangers, water recycling) and distribution (e.g. supply chains, storage, safety, traceability, transport efficiency). As a result, the temporal and spatial relevancy of inventories and methodologies need to be considered when evaluating and using past work. For example, a national GHG footprint of the US dry-mill ethanol industry from the 1980s would not be representative of a newly constructed, 450 million liter (120 million gallon) per year wet-mill ethanol biorefinery in Iowa because of changes in milling processes. However, this older evaluation could provide a valuable resource to document improvements. When used appropriately LCAs can be a tool to improve efficiency and sustainability of production (Notarnicola, Salomone et al. 2015, Poore and Nemecek 2018).

Reference source not found.) are typically included in an LCA study: (1) The scope, including the system boundary and level of detail of an LCA, depends on the subject and the intended use of the study; (2) The Life Cycle Inventory analysis phase (LCI phase) is the second phase of LCA; it is an inventory of input/output data about the individual processes (or stages) of the system being studied. It involves the collection of the data necessary to meet the goals of the defined study, (3) The Life Cycle Impact Assessment phase (LCIA) is the third phase of the LCA. The purpose of LCIA is to provide additional information to help assess a product system's LCI results to better understand their environmental significance. 5: <header>2.5. Types of LCAs </header>

The level of detail which goes into an LCA varies and is used to classify LCAs into several broad categories. Most people are familiar with the term 'footprint'. A product footprint is a common but highly simplified LCA which considers only one factor in the impact analysis, such as water or carbon. Footprints were the first type of LCA from which the standards and methods we use today evolved due to the shortcomings of considering only one trait within a system. For example, in 1997 the Kyoto Protocol initiated international emissions trading and clean development mechanisms which stimulated development of some now defunct voluntary carbon marketplaces, such as the Chicago Climate Exchange. One of the lessons learned during that period was that crediting only one component, such as soil carbon sequestration alone without considering GHG emissions (e.g., N2O from fertilizer, CO2 from fossil fuel use), created incomplete analyses and did not create an offset that was well valued within a voluntary marketplace. To create value and improve sustainability - emission reductions, offsets, credits, and environmental accounting practices need to address systemic life cycle effects. Many types of LCAs have been developed to fulfill that need, but the most accepted broad categories are consequential and attributional (Weidema, Pizzol et al. 2018). Few evaluations fit cleanly into one category or another (McManus and Taylor 2015). Consequential LCAs are system-based models which often are national in scale and include many indirect effects. These LCA analyses address not only the inputs and outputs of a system, but also the consequences of subsequent use and production. Consequential LCAs can be useful in policy development, for example, they can be used to evaluate the effect of biofuel and bioenergy policies supporting the

introduction of more industrial crops into food crop rotations (Styles, Gibbons et al. 2015). Whereas, attributional LCAs are detailed, primarily process-specific, and frequently only consider direct effects. Attributional LCA can be useful to companies in evaluating and improving production processes and factors such as supply chains, material sourcing, and marketing. This type of LCA is also useful to consumers and decision-makers to compare products or processes and answer questions, such as which biorefinery has the lowest water use per liter (gallon) of ethanol produced? Many agricultural LCAs are attributional analyses because the goal and scope of these analyses are often focused on improving or evaluating a specific farm product or source in comparison to past practices or competitors, not to evaluate the consequences of the entire production system.

7: <header>2.6. Terminology </header>

Many similar acronyms and terms are used within LCAs and can create confusion, particularly, because some acronyms can have multiple meanings (Table 2.1). As a result, it is advisable to examine each individual evaluation or regulation for guidance.
Table 2.1. Definitions of key terms and abbreviations that are useful to know.

Term|Non-technical Definition Allocation|Method of distributing benefits and burdens among multiple products from a process. Attributional|A life cycle analysis approach which looks at the direct burdens of specific product or portion of a life cycle. Background|Foundational or intermediate systems supporting seed cultivar or breed development, new livestock generations (hatcheries, herd systems, etc.), and agroecosystem hierarchy required to produce farm and field system inputs. By-product|Marketable products produced as a result of the production process with less value than main products.

Term|Non-technical Definition Consequential|A life cycle analysis approach which addresses the indirect and direct burdens which occur as a result of a decision, and it is usually based on economic models. Co-product|Marketable suite of products produced as a result of the production process (e.g., dried distillers' grains) which are integral to production economics. Damage Assessment|An impact assessment methodology estimates longer-term, cumulative, systemic, or societal losses to systems which support life on Earth. Endpoint|The systemic impacts of environmental releases to humans, societies, and or ecosystems (e.g., mortality of pollution). Equivalent|Within impact analyses, it would be overwhelming if all emissions were individually defined. To aggregate and simplify the results, equivalents based on impact severity (e.g., toxicity, hazard potential) are used to aggregate different environmental releases. Footprint|Characterization of a single environmental impact or resource consumption along the supply chain of a product. Foreground|System components which are prominent or closest contributors to the product, process, or system being analyzed. Functional Unit|The unit for products or services at which LCA results are presented. GHG|Greenhouse Gases: Gases that contribute to global climate change by absorbing infrared radiation within the atmosphere. Agriculturally- related LCAs usually account for carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), and nitrous oxide (N20). ISO|International Standards Organization: An independent, non- governmental collaboration of national standards organizations and regulators which develop voluntary, consensus-based international guidelines. LCA|Life Cycle Assessment or Analysis: An accounting technique to evaluate environmental impacts associated with products, processes, or systems from raw material sources to end of use, disposal, or consumption. LCC|Life Cycle Costing: An accounting technique to evaluate the fiscal impacts associated with products, processes, or systems from raw material sources to end of use, disposal, or consumption. LCI|Life Cycle Inventory: A summary of materials and energy used to produce a product or suite of products. LCI|Life Cycle Interpretation: A summary of the results of inventory and impact analyses.

Term|Non-technical Definition LCIA|Life Cycle Impact Assessment or Analysis: An accounting method which quantifies and weighs environmental impacts. LCT|Life Cycle Thinking: A framework which is used to holistically evaluate systems and the implications of the production and consumption of products or energy. LUC|Land Use Change: An estimate of systemic human-caused conversions in land use due to product production (e.g., grasslands or forests converted to row crop production). Meta-analysis|Statistical analysis which evaluates the results of multiple previously conducted studies. Midpoint|Compartmentalized impacts of releases and activities on the environment, e.g., air quality impacts of air pollutant emissions. Pedigree|Recorded, past uncertainty contribution estimates due to data collection methods, sourcing, and confidence. Primary data|Information which is directly collected by a researcher. Registry|A designated voluntary or regulatory authority which records and tracks production and retirement of emissions reductions, allowances, or offsets. Scope|The defined system being evaluated, the primary research questions being answered and analytical boundaries of the evaluation. Substitution|Replacement of a higher value or greater impact product with a lower value, demand, or impact alternative. Supply Chain|Systems which support the movement and transport of materials and resources. System Boundary|A definition of what is included or excluded from the analysis. Unit Process|The smallest element for which data within an inventory analysis is quantified.

11: <header>2.7. System boundaries </header>
An LCA can evaluate different components of a system or the entire system.

When an LCA covers all aspects of a product's life cycle, it is called a cradle-to-grave analysis (Figure 2.1). A cradle-to-grave analysis evaluates everything from raw material sourcing to disposal or consumption. For example, an attributional LCA of can of cola would evaluate all the resources, ingredients, and packaging used as well as processes from grain production to processing stages, such as making corn syrup, through to cola

consumption and can recycling. With agricultural products, much of the burden of production occurs on the farm. As a result, cradle-to-farm-gate analyses are common (e.g., Kebreab, Liedke et al. 2016). The LCA system boundary for a given product is very different between a consequential LCA and an attributional LCA.
12: <header>2.7.1. Crop system boundaries </header>

Evaluation of crop-based products is typically focused on the impact of crop production. The results of crop production LCAs can vary depending on the data sources and aggregation methods, production methods and equipment, yields, and analytical assumptions. Using LCA to evaluate the role that a crop has within rotational cropping systems is an emerging area of research. Crop LCA evaluations normally focus on direct field inputs and activities such as water, seed, fertilizer, fuel, electricity, pesticide, and land use, as well as tillage practices (Figure 2.6). Because crops are produced to supply a wide variety of products, farm-gate-to-grave processes and impacts can vary substantially. For example, the farm-gate carbon intensity based on data from Flugge, Lewandrowski et al. (2017) is ~40% lower (Figures 2.3 & 2.4; 1940 kg CO2e ha-1) than that of Pelton (2019) (Figure 2.6; 3309 kg CO2e ha-1) due primarily to differences in geographic aggregation and inclusion of lime in the analyses. Traditionally, LCAs have focused on national-scale evaluations, however, there has been increased interest in creating regional- or smaller-scale evaluations to explore and address geographic differences in production. Many LCA evaluations are place- and scale-oriented and may or may not be applicable to other locations or scales. Evaluation of indirect impacts is variable and dependent on the 'downstream' end use and market. 'Upstream' impacts,

such as raw materials used in equipment manufacturing or building construction, are often negligible within attributional LCAs if used to support production of multiple farm products over an extended period.

Figure 2.6. Field gate US national average emissions for corn production of approximately 0.35 kg CO2e kg-1 (9.0 kg CO2e bu-1) of corn grain from Pelton (2019) shown as a Sankey diagram. Pelton (2019) found that corn production impacts ranged from 0.20 to 0.70 kg CO2e kg-1 (4.9 to 17.7 kg CO2e bu-1) of corn grain across the US. Nitrogen fertilizer manufacturing and N2O emissions associated with use contribute significantly to US corn production GHG emissions.
13: <header>2.7.1. Crop system boundaries </header>

Evaluation of crop-based products is typically focused on the impact of crop production. The results of crop production LCAs can vary depending on the data sources and aggregation methods, production methods and equipment, yields, and analytical assumptions. Using LCA to evaluate the role that a crop has within rotational cropping systems is an emerging area of research. Crop LCA evaluations normally focus on direct field inputs and activities such as water, seed, fertilizer, fuel, electricity, pesticide, and land use, as well as tillage practices (Figure 2.6). Because crops are produced to supply a wide variety of products, farm-gate-to-grave processes and impacts can vary substantially. For example, the farm-gate carbon intensity based on data from Flugge, Lewandrowski et al. (2017) is ~40% lower (Figures 2.3 & 2.4; 1940 kg CO2e ha-1) than that of Pelton (2019) (Figure 2.6; 3309 kg CO2e ha-1) due primarily to differences in geographic aggregation and inclusion of lime in the analyses. Traditionally, LCAs have focused on national-scale evaluations, however, there has been increased interest in creating regional- or smaller-scale evaluations to explore and address geographic differences in production. Many LCA evaluations are place- and scale-oriented and may or may not be applicable to other locations or scales. Evaluation of indirect impacts is variable and dependent on the 'downstream' end use and market. 'Upstream' impacts,

such as raw materials used in equipment manufacturing or building construction, are often negligible within attributional LCAs if used to support production of multiple farm products over an extended period.

14: The process considered for animal systems in cradle-to-farm-gate system boundaries normally include feed production, animal husbandry, manure storage and farm management. Farm-gate-to-grave systems include processes such as transportation of product from farm to processing plants, manufacturing of packing materials such as bottles for milk and cartons for eggs, and distribution to retailers and consumers. Activities considered 'upstream' such as the extraction of raw materials for fuels and intermediate products required in the system such as diesel, electricity, and fertilizer are
not usually included. In addition, there may be activities considered to be 'downstream' such as waste processing that may not be included within the system boundary (Figure 2.7).

Figure 2.7. Life cycle summary of primary system inputs and outputs for swine production created using data from Kebreab, Liedke et al. (2016). Direct pig production inventories, emissions, and resource uses were reallocated to represent one average live weight pig of 130 kg (287 lb) from a US production scenario without amino acid or phytase supplementation based LCAs by Kebreab, Liedke et al. (2016) on global livestock production.

16: The boundaries for biofuel systems can be more complex because they also must account for biorefinery processing and associated impacts in addition to that of the agricultural system (Thornley and Adams 2018). Most modern biorefineries integrate an array of high-value bioproducts, not just biofuels, and have a much higher efficiency than their predecessors (Ray and Ramachandaran 2018). Particularly with biofuels, policy- related requirements, modeling assumptions, analysis limitations, and software can have a significant effect on results (Hoekman and Broch 2018, Obnamia, Dias et al. 2019, Agostini, Giuntoli et al. 2020). As a result, users need to carefully consider the context,

usefulness, and applicability of these complex analyses. Many biofuel life cycle accounting policies and studies try to evaluate the changes that biofuel production has on food production systems. To evaluate impacts to production systems, most analyses use some metric to first assess the status quo such as a reference system (Hoekman and Broch 2018), business-as-us-usual (BAU) scenario, or baseline, and then quantify changes that biofuel production creates. As a result, most biofuel-related system boundaries (Figure 2.8) also include quantifications which involve product substitution or displacement (Vadenbo, Hellweg et al. 2017). Inclusion of these factors are critical to properly applying more advanced allocation methods capable of addressing biofuel production system complexities. The nuances of these concepts can be hard to grasp; but it is important to understand the core principles. Think of it as a recipe in your grandma's old cookbook which calls for lard. In LCA terms, the recipe would be the BAU or point of reference to evaluate change. We now know that eating lard is not healthy for us. So, to adapt the recipe, you can substitute the lard with an omega-6 rich oil which we now know to be a healthier alternative to support other dietary changes you are making to improve your overall health. The reasons, cumulative changes, and effects on your heart-health can be complex. Life cycle analyses work the same way, only through them we attempt to quantify the systemic effect of these changes.
Figure 2.8. Generalized system boundary diagram for cellulosic ethanol production system using pathways within GREET (DOE 2019).

19: The selection of functional units used in LCAs typically is closely related to the evaluation's goals and frequently is expressed as a unit of area (e.g., hectare or acre) or mass (e.g., kg or bushel of grain, kg or lb of meat) for food-related LCAs. For biofuel- related LCAs an energy unit such as megajoules (MJ) or British thermal units (Btu) typically is used because energy units facilitate comparting LCA results to those from fossil fuel or other biofuel types (Figure 2.9). Functional units are needed to compare resource use and production impacts. Agricultural LCAs are designed to be quantitative, however, the value of agricultural products is often qualitative (e.g., protein, oil, or moisture contents or food/carcass grades), varies significantly over time, and is judged differently across markets. So, care must be taken, particularly with respect to functional units, when using and apply LCA results from different markets.

0: <title>Corn-Stover Ethanol Upper Midwest Average Greenhouse Gas Distribution Using Data from Kim et al. (2019) for Non-RFS Compliant Systems </title>

Figure 2.9. Average life cycle contributions to corn stover-based ethanol production modified from Kim, Dale et al. (2019) for non-RFS (US Renewable Fuel Standard 40 CFR Part 80) compliant biofuel production systems. Compliant RFS corn stover-based ethanol facilities within Kim, Dale et al. (2019) had life cycle emissions ranging from 21.8 to 37.2 g CO2e MJ-1. In this visualization, electricity credits due to on-site power generation were proportionately distributed across all ethanol production impacts.
1: Models like LCA are used to better understand complex systems. It is important to remember that the model results or outputs do not necessarily represent individual processes or emissions. Many LCA model outputs (Table 2.2) represent global averages, cumulated or collective impacts, and equivalent pollutant or contaminant effects, which LCA practitioners commonly call 'impacts' and 'damage assessments'. So, the directly measured effects are translated into a common value based on aggregated outputs used to group likely effects to simplify and better understand system-wide environmental processes. For example, when GHG emissions are evaluated, the functional unit is defined in terms of the 100-year carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2 eq.) based on radiative forcing and residence time of individual compounds. Carbon equivalents allows equitable comparison among greenhouse gases (i.e., CO2, CH4, N2O, SF6, CFCs, and

HCFCs/HFCs), which are actively monitored for their effect on the global climate change (IPCC 2006). Greenhouse gases differ in atmospheric residence time and in their global warming potential. Thus, calculating their emission or stocks as carbon equivalents allows equitable comparison of their respective impact on climate changes. However, an emission of a given compound or resource consumption can affect more than one environmental process. An example of a gas emission which effects multiple processes would be chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs); this grouping of several similar gases affects both climate change and ozone in the upper atmosphere. As a result of CFC emissions, outputs could be calculated in terms of CO2 eq. to represent the climate change impact and CFC-11 eq. to represent contributions to ozone depletion.
Table 2.2. List of common agricultural management factors and common outputs likely affected.

4: <header>2.10. Allocation </header>

Two related key factors to identify in agricultural LCAs is allocation and product handling. Allocation is the method of distribution of benefits and burdens of production among different products. Basic allocation methods are based on quantity, quality, energy, or value, whereas, more complex ones are based on equivalency (i.e. cereal units, substitution) or additionality, such as system expansion. Within system expansion allocation, the burden of new co-products is only the changes to the existing systems needed to produce the new products. Some regulations require a specific allocation method, however, in many instances there is no right or wrong allocation system as long as it is well-described in the evaluation.

The terms used when referencing multiple products denote how the burdens and benefits of production are shared within the analysis: co-product, by-product, and waste. Co-products share some allocated production burden or benefit. By-products are low- value products and do not significantly share in the allocated responsibility for production. Wastes are a burden of production and may have an associated disposal responsibility. This categorization (co- versus by-product or waste) can change over time or regionally due to changes in market development supply, value, and abundance.

Farming systems are based on natural processes which have been harnessed to produce specific products. As an extension of natural systems, resources are cycled if the agroecosystem remains balanced which results in minimal wastes. Farms can be fully self-sufficient with minimal resources imported or exported, which in terms of LCAs, would have no by-products because resources are fully recycled. However, self- sufficient farms are rare in modern agriculture due to global urbanization and demand for

agricultural products. The product handling in LCAs is especially important when agricultural (i.e. stover, straw), forestry (i.e. slash, trimmings), or livestock residues (i.e. urine, manure, tallow) are used. Consideration of agricultural residues as either a co- product or by-product will allocate it a share of the benefits and burdens whereas consideration as waste will not; residue allocation can substantially increase or decrease the life cycle impact of crop residue use.

7: <header>2.11. Agricultural special inventory considerations </header>

Soil carbon sequestration has the potential to be a valued sink for GHG emissions. However, the complexities of soil carbon dynamics require careful evaluation and consideration of sequestration rates and benefits. As soils aggrade (e.g., build or increase) and accumulate organic matter, the densities and thicknesses of soil horizons change. Accumulation rates vary with both climate and agronomic management. As a result, short-term depth-based measurements can be more reflective of recent weather trends, crop rotations, density changes, or sampling methods than actual increases in stable soil carbon. Long-term, particularly no-till, studies that only look at the uppermost soil horizon(s) can also be misrepresentative because soil carbon present at depth due to past tillage can be redistributed near the surface as more natural soil horizons are re- established. When soil carbon sequestration is considered in LCAs, well-replicated long- term soil equivalent mass basis measurements and models are needed to represent changes to the soil carbon pools. Neither models nor measurements alone are adequate to represent the variability and dynamics of natural systems. Models need measurements for calibration and validation, while measurements need models to account for spatial and
temporal variations. Together, they can improve the accuracy of life cycle inventories and model simulations.

Geographically, agricultural production systems vary due to climatic conditions, culture, policy, and industrialization. The carrying capacity and productivity of agricultural lands are strongly affected by both past and present agricultural systems. The demand for food, fiber, fuel, and feed is increasing while the amount of productive, arable land is decreasing. To maintain growth, innovations and changes must be made to continue to increase agricultural productivity while both meeting consumer demands for quality and well-being and improving the equitability of food distribution (Hasegawa, Havlík et al. 2019). It is important to consider these improvements and fundamental differences when using and interpreting LCAs.

Few farmers produce only one crop. Crops are not independently produced, as each crop is part of an agronomic system which, if well-designed, provides beneficial biofeedbacks and efficient use of natural resources to maximize production. It is often cost- and time-prohibitive to conduct whole farm LCAs - necessitating individual crop LCAs. For example, a biofuel company is purchasing corn in bulk from multiple regional elevators and does not know the crop rotations or practices used. The biorefinery needs a GHG footprint for their certification, but because the corn cannot be easily traced back to a source, the best option would be to use regional statistics to represent typical farm practices. Therefore, it is important to place these evaluations within appropriate agroecological context when interpreting or applying results. Additionally, it is critical to make sure sound experimental designs have been used and key agronomic factors have been considered. When trying to evaluate an agronomic

management practice or bioproduct with an LCA (especially when dealing with limited information from short-term or small-plot trials) it is important to closely examine the data and experimental designs and ask critical questions, such as: is the data representative? Are all critical agronomic factors included? Are these results repeatable? If the answer is to any of these questions is no, then the LCA results are likely not going to be meaningful.

Crops are not independently produced, as each crop is part of an agronomic system which, if well-designed, provides beneficial biofeedbacks and efficient use of natural resources to maximize production. It is often cost- and time-prohibitive to conduct whole farm LCAs - necessitating individual crop LCAs. For example, a biofuel company is purchasing corn in bulk from multiple regional elevators and does not know the crop rotations or practices used. The biorefinery needs a GHG footprint for their certification, but because the corn cannot be easily traced back to a source, the best option would be to use regional statistics to represent typical farm practices. Therefore, it is important to place these evaluations within appropriate agroecological context when interpreting or applying results. Additionally, it is critical to make sure sound experimental designs have been used and key agronomic factors have been considered. When trying to evaluate an agronomic

management practice or bioproduct with an LCA (especially when dealing with limited information from short-term or small-plot trials) it is important to closely examine the data and experimental designs and ask critical questions, such as: is the data representative? Are all critical agronomic factors included? Are these results repeatable? If the answer is to any of these questions is no, then the LCA results are likely not going to be meaningful.
10: <header>2.11. Agricultural special inventory considerations </header>

Soil carbon sequestration has the potential to be a valued sink for GHG emissions. However, the complexities of soil carbon dynamics require careful evaluation and consideration of sequestration rates and benefits. As soils aggrade (e.g., build or increase) and accumulate organic matter, the densities and thicknesses of soil horizons change. Accumulation rates vary with both climate and agronomic management. As a result, short-term depth-based measurements can be more reflective of recent weather trends, crop rotations, density changes, or sampling methods than actual increases in stable soil carbon. Long-term, particularly no-till, studies that only look at the uppermost soil horizon(s) can also be misrepresentative because soil carbon present at depth due to past tillage can be redistributed near the surface as more natural soil horizons are re- established. When soil carbon sequestration is considered in LCAs, well-replicated long- term soil equivalent mass basis measurements and models are needed to represent changes to the soil carbon pools. Neither models nor measurements alone are adequate to represent the variability and dynamics of natural systems. Models need measurements for calibration and validation, while measurements need models to account for spatial and
temporal variations. Together, they can improve the accuracy of life cycle inventories and model simulations.

Geographically, agricultural production systems vary due to climatic conditions, culture, policy, and industrialization. The carrying capacity and productivity of agricultural lands are strongly affected by both past and present agricultural systems. The demand for food, fiber, fuel, and feed is increasing while the amount of productive, arable land is decreasing. To maintain growth, innovations and changes must be made to continue to increase agricultural productivity while both meeting consumer demands for quality and well-being and improving the equitability of food distribution (Hasegawa, Havlík et al. 2019). It is important to consider these improvements and fundamental differences when using and interpreting LCAs.

Few farmers produce only one crop. Crops are not independently produced, as each crop is part of an agronomic system which, if well-designed, provides beneficial biofeedbacks and efficient use of natural resources to maximize production. It is often cost- and time-prohibitive to conduct whole farm LCAs - necessitating individual crop LCAs. For example, a biofuel company is purchasing corn in bulk from multiple regional elevators and does not know the crop rotations or practices used. The biorefinery needs a GHG footprint for their certification, but because the corn cannot be easily traced back to a source, the best option would be to use regional statistics to represent typical farm practices. Therefore, it is important to place these evaluations within appropriate agroecological context when interpreting or applying results. Additionally, it is critical to make sure sound experimental designs have been used and key agronomic factors have been considered. When trying to evaluate an agronomic

management practice or bioproduct with an LCA (especially when dealing with limited information from short-term or small-plot trials) it is important to closely examine the data and experimental designs and ask critical questions, such as: is the data representative? Are all critical agronomic factors included? Are these results repeatable? If the answer is to any of these questions is no, then the LCA results are likely not going to be meaningful.
11: <header>2.12. Impact assessment methodologies </header>

The impact assessment methodologies are systems which assess the likely effect the modeled system and inventory have on the environment, such as GHG emissions, water use, and pollution. Some are single-impact methodologies and others evaluate a suite of likely impacts and damages. Specialized software tools, such as GaBI (thinkstep, Chicago, IL, US), SimaPro (Pré Consultants, the Netherlands, EU), Umberto (ifu hamburg, Germany, EU), GREET (Argonne National Laboratory, Lemont, IL, US), and OpenLCA (GreenDelta GmbH, Germany, EU) have multiple varying preprogrammed impact assessment methodologies and packages available. There can be subtle differences in the calculation methodologies and software tools, which can affect results. So, the impact assessment methodology and toolset should be selected to best address the region and project goals. Creating appropriate, representative analyses which can be clearly followed, described, and replicated and meets standards and regulations is often more important than the tools. However, the results of LCAs are only as good as the inventory and inputs used. If an inventory does not include key factors, such as nitrate lost in 1 runoff to streams, the result is meaningless.

An overwhelming number of specialized impact assessment methodologies and suites of model outputs have been developed (UNEP and SETAC 2016). The appropriateness of an impact assessment varies (JRC and IES 2011), for example it is common to use the US EPA's method, TRACI (Tool of Reduction and Assessment of Chemicals and Other Environmental Impacts) for US-focused LCAs (EPA 2006) whereas analyses with an international scope commonly use ReCiPe which can reflect a global or international market perspective (iPoint-systems gmbh 2018). Impact assessment methods generally fall into two types based on 1) midpoints which focus on individual issues or impacts, such as GHGs, ecotoxicity, or nutrient pollution (i.e. eutrophication) and 2) endpoints which evaluate the collective influence of multiple issues to create weighted scores (e.g., Eq. 2.1) to assess damages or effects on specific systems. In addition, many proprietary and governmental software tools and inventory libraries are available (WRI and wbcsd 2019). Prior to selection, it is important to first better understand LCA's core principals. The United Nations provides free guidance, resources, and training materials (lifecycleinitiative.org) which can assist in LCA-related decision making, learning more about how LCA can be used, and tools and data internationally available for both global and regional evaluations through their Global LCA Data network (GLAD).

13: <header>W1.GHGs + w2'ecotoxicity + w3*eutrophication = Wscore </header>
Where: W = weight
Many modern environmental accounting softwares originated as GHG-focused spreadsheet tools or resources which continue to evolve, grow, and change to meet

industry and governmental needs (Arioli, D'Agosto et al. 2020). BioGrace (biograce.net) and GHGenius (ghgenius.ca) are examples of free, spreadsheet-based biofuel GHG calculation tools which can be used to meet some regulatory requirements (Pereira, Cavalett et al. 2019). Other resources, such as GREET (greet.es.anl.gov) started as a US spreadsheet-based GHG accounting tool but has also evolved into a more advanced biofuel-focused LCA software. These types of free resources can serve as a good introductory platform for people wanting to learn more about agriculture-related or biofuel life cycle accounting.
14: All agricultural LCAs contain assumptions which are simplifications of reality. Assumptions are unavoidable because of the inherent heterogeneity of agroecosystems. Therefore, it is important to assess the confidence and quality of both the input data and the assumptions built into the models (Del Grosso, Ogle et al. 2010). Confidence in model assumptions can be quantified by comparing model outputs with measurements from research plots. Confidence in input data can be determined through use of various statistical modeling techniques, commonly a Monte Carlo analysis. A Monte Carlo analysis varies each input value based on a distribution provided to the software to represent a credible spectrum of outcomes.

With agricultural LCAs, models are often sensitive to yield, fertilizer use, enteric and manure emissions, and fuel use. However, the sensitivity of each model should be explored at minimum either through a structured analysis or scenario development. Sensitivity analyses are used to understand the effect model inputs have on analytical

results. It is important to always consider the limitations and representativeness of any analysis. Management changes which alter these key sensitivity factors may impact one or more output within the impact or damage assessment.
15: It is not always easy to compare results from one LCA to another due to differences in the accounting methods. When comparing results, the first step is to consider the geographic setting, agricultural systems, and climates and determine the usefulness of the comparisons. Trade-offs and fundamental differences between agricultural production systems are unavoidable. The reality is that no farming or ranching system is perfect. Within agroecosystems, diversity provides resiliency. When making cross-system or global comparisons, it is important to consider how we can continue to reduce the impacts of agricultural production and trade-offs within each field, farm, region, or nation within the confines of natural resources available and to systemically improve agroecological resiliency. A one-size-fits-all agronomic solution does not exist. Even within efficient production systems, additional improvements can always be made; therefore, analytical comparisons should reflect and respect that diversity and on-going change.
16: Standards have been developed to ensure the quality and comparability of analyses and products. Robust standards can assist in meeting regulatory requirements for globally marketed products and are used to deter analytical misrepresentation and bias. Numerous guidelines are available. Most modern LCAs and many regulations have

been developed based on one or more international standard. The most widely applied international standards are produced by the International Standards Organization (ISO; iso.org). These standards provide loose guidelines on LCA methodology (ISO 14040, 14072), required elements (ISO 14044, 14047), labeling (ISO 14025, 21930), analysis context (ISO 14047), selection (ISO 14049), and evaluation (ISO 14071) (Abraham 2017). Most LCAs follow one or more of the ISO standards, in addition, various other resources and guidelines are available for specific products or markets. Much can be learned from using different approaches, often the specific standard does not matter as much as the fact that one was consistently followed. Analyses which do not follow a standardized guideline are less likely to meet regulatory or industry requirements and are more likely to contain bias. Agricultural products are a critical foundational element of national and international stability and trade, as a result, individual standards have been developed for specific key products.

Livestock are a critical wealth-building asset for developing countries and a globally important protein source which has a significant environmental impact. The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) brought together a multi stakeholder initiative under the Livestock Environmental Assessment and Performance Partnership (LEAP) (fao.org/partnerships/leap) to benchmark and monitor environmental performance of the livestock sector using LCA (FAO 2019). The LCA methodology and guidance developed by LEAP are intended to be used by stakeholders in all countries and across the entire range of livestock production systems. In developing the guidelines, it was assumed that the primary users will be individuals or organizations with a good working knowledge of LCA. The main purpose of the guidelines was to define

calculation methods and data requirements on quality and transparency to enable consistent application of LCA across differing livestock supply chains. So far, LEAP has published LCA guidelines for environmental performance of animal feeds, large ruminant and pig supply chains, greenhouse gas emissions and fossil energy demand from poultry and small ruminant supply chains, biodiversity and nutrient flows, and associated environmental impacts in livestock supply chains (fao.org/partnerships/leap/publications). Another LEAP guideline that allows for comparison of scenarios with and without specific feed additives and combinations thereof is under development.

The benefits of LEAP approach include (1) the use of a recognized, robust and transparent methodology developed to take account of the nature of livestock supply chains (2) the identification of supply chain hotspots and opportunities to improve and reduce environmental impact (3) the estimation of efficiency and productivity increases (4) the provision of support for reporting and communication requirements; and (5) awareness raising and supporting action on environmental sustainability. The LEAP guidelines are based on the attributional approach to life cycle accounting.

20: Life cycle analyses and results are applied in laws and policies in many ways (Lehmann, Finkbeiner et al. 2015). It is important for analyses to follow pertinent regulatory guidelines within the intended market jurisdiction. Regulatory requirements change [see GRI (2019)], SO periodic checks and methodological updates can be necessary to maintain compliance. Some regulations, such as the US RFS2, certify

pathways whereas others, such as the EU Renewable Energy Directive have a greater focus on feedstocks (EU 2009, EPA 2010). Many regulations, companies, and industrial sectors have progressive targets where requirements commonly become more stringent over time.
21: The goal of some LCAs is to meet legislative requirements or to generate emission reductions or offsets. An emission reduction can be a regulatory mandate or voluntary pledge where the environmental impact due a product or process has been documented and can be certified to be lower than it was previously or to meet regulatory targets. An offset is an emission reduction which can be purchased in lieu of direct emission reductions. Many emission reductions and offsets are applicable to a specified window of time, such as the year the offset occurred, but can be available for many years before they are used and retired. If a company reduces emissions more than their regulatory requirement, these emission reductions can often be sold to companies which were unable to do SO. Emission reductions and offsets are the basis for many market- based environmental compliance systems. Some governments, have opted to create environmental taxes, emissions trading systems, cap and trade markets, allowances, public-private partnerships, or auctions. As of 2018, there are 88 international, 45 national, and 25 sub-national (e.g., regional, state, providence, or district) carbon pricing initiatives and over 1,300 companies setting internal performance goals and standards (World Bank 2018). Widespread adoption of carbon programs by nations, states
(provinces), and industry has created an environmental market worth more than $82 billion dollars globally in 2018 for carbon alone (World Bank 2018).

Many policies require a verification process for emission reductions or offsets. The intensity of verification varies and can range from an auditor conducting full in person checks to a random sampling of off-site paperwork checks. Verification can be a lengthy or costly process, or it can be as simple as a pledge of honesty. The verification process requirements are typically set by governing agencies and/or purchasers. Companies can set a more stringent verification process than regulatory requirements to increase product value and marketability.
22: <header>2.16.1. Emission reductions and offsets </header>

The goal of some LCAs is to meet legislative requirements or to generate emission reductions or offsets. An emission reduction can be a regulatory mandate or voluntary pledge where the environmental impact due a product or process has been documented and can be certified to be lower than it was previously or to meet regulatory targets. An offset is an emission reduction which can be purchased in lieu of direct emission reductions. Many emission reductions and offsets are applicable to a specified window of time, such as the year the offset occurred, but can be available for many years before they are used and retired. (provinces), and industry has created an environmental market worth more than $82 billion dollars globally in 2018 for carbon alone (World Bank 2018).
