23: <header>2.17. How do I get started doing a footprint or LCA? </header>

Life cycle analyses are transdisciplinary tool which can and are conducted by people in nearly every profession. This is because subject-matter expertise is often needed to help with system understanding and interpreting the results. As a result, the best and most useful analyses are not conducted by individuals, but rather teams with a diverse set of skills and backgrounds that can tackle complex environmental, economic, and social problems. Adding your subject matter expertise to an LCA project can be a good introduction to the steps and processes that need to be learned SO that you can conduct your own analyses or lead an LCA team in the future.

For most agronomy-related projects, the first and most important step is to determine what questions you would like to answer and your analytical goals. Once you have this accomplished, take a step back and try to get a handle on the scope of work. Do you need a basic or general understanding of only one metric, such as water, energy, or

GHGs? If so, then an online search for a footprint calculator may provide you with relationship and impact answers that you need. If you need a project- or process-specific understanding or an evaluation of trade-offs, then more in-depth analysis, resources, and tools are likely needed. Whether you are planning to do the analysis yourself or working with an LCA professional or team, you will likely need to gather data and build an inventory. Do you have the experimental or statistical data to answer these questions? If you do not, is it available from the past work of others in publications or LCA inventories? It is important to develop a good plan and ensure that resources are available prior to starting an LCA, once you have done so, the primary considerations (see Figure 5) and resources listed earlier in this primer can be used to help you in developing your own LCAs.

25: <header>2.18. Summary </header>

As agricultural production continues to evolve and advance, LCAs will likely play a key role in documenting, tracking, and marketing improvements. It is important to be aware of the uses, limitations, and benefits LCAs provide. Numerous public resources, such as the United Nations Life Cycle Initiative (lifecycleinitiative.org), are available to the agricultural community to learn more about LCAs, data resources available through Global LCA Data network (GLAD), livestock-specific resources such as LEAP, and assessment results. Agricultural products are critical resources which form the foundation of civilization. As such, the impact of agricultural products and their production system is important to understand and evaluate. LCAs can be used to identify
production strengths and weaknesses, which in turn, can be used to improve agroecosystem sustainability.
26: This work was supported by the South Dakota Corn Utilization Council. Argonne National Laboratory's work was supported by the US Department of Energy, Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, Bioenergy Technologies Office under contract DE-AC02-06CH11357.
27: Heidi Sieverding drafted the text for all parts of the paper and created all figures. The text was reviewed and modified by co-authors.
28: Are agriculture-based emission reductions and offsets permanent? Agricultural products are transitive in nature (e.g., milk spoils, grain molds, oil becomes rancid). Food, feed, fiber, feedstocks, and biofuels have a finite lifespan unless altered or preserved under tightly controlled conditions that curtail microbial or physical decay. Nature and natural processes are cyclic and ever-changing. Yet, the life cycle analytical approach uses permanence as a standard by which agricultural-based emission offsets and reductions are held (Ruseva, Hedrick et al. 2020).

Soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration in agricultural lands is a GHG offset mechanism in the European Union, Canada, and many other countries, but not within the US. This is in some part, because US farmers and policymakers have past, negative experiences with soil carbon sequestration offsets from the Chicago Climate Exchange (CCX) era. However, the primary reason is a lack of scientific consensus on permanence.

Direct CO2 and GHG removal schemes, and "climate-smart" production practices and products are a popular component of emission reduction targets and strategies (Gardezi, Michael et al. 2021, Abbass, Qasim et al. 2022). The immediate, intermediate, and long-term benefits of specific climate mitigation strategies become uncertain upon close examination. The climate forcing of a GHG is not different because of its source; the atmospheric impact is the same whether CO2 is from a person, plant, biofuel, or fossil fuel. To be successful in stalling or reversing climate change - real, measurable changes in atmospheric composition are needed (IPCC 2023). Soils gain carbon and lose carbon continuously through vegetative growth and decay. For SOC sequestration to be accepted as permanent, the dynamic stability of soil needs to be recognized as a form of

permanence (Dynarski, Bossio et al. 2020). For the scientific community to accept and incorporate soil organic carbon sequestration, the measured effect of individual agricultural practices on a global scale needs to be systematically documented and analyzed. A method of accomplishing this systemic documentation and analysis is via a meta-analysis.

A series of three meta-analyses were conducted to evaluate soil carbon changes due to use of crop residues, cover crops, and manure/compost amendments when producing corn. These three farm management strategies are commonly used methods farmers use to build soils while maintaining a non-perennial cropping system. Corn stover and other crop residues are typically retained on US fields, however, this is not a practice used world-wide. In many developing nations, all crop residue is removed and used as livestock bedding or forage, or burned to make planting the next crop easier (Clay, Alverson et al. 2019). One of the feedstocks commonly being used for cellulosic ethanol production is corn stover (Alavijeh, Shahvandi et al. 2023). In the future, other residues from high residue yielding crops such as sorghum (Sorghum bicolor) (Yang, Dahlberg et al. 2021) or hemp (Cannabis sativa) (Zhao, Xu et al. 2020, Griffin 2022) will likely be used as cellulosic biofuel feedstocks. The sustainable removal of corn stover and crop residues is an important consideration for sustainable crop, cellulosic biofuel, and advanced bioproduct production. Xu, Sieverding et al. 2019 focused on evaluating the global impact of corn stover removal on soil organic carbon stocks. The meta-analysis used 409 paired datasets from 74 stover harvest experiments. This study found that stover removal reduced soil carbon sequestration rates, but, with careful management, moderate levels of corn stover removal (<50%) could be sustainably removed (Xu, Sieverding et al.

2019). Joshi, Sieverding et al. 2023 evaluated the effect of cover-cropping on SOC sequestration in corn productions systems. This global meta-analysis used 198 paired datasets from 61 peer-reviewed publications. Adding cover-crops to corn production systems increased SOC sequestration by 7.3%, particularly, for soils with low initial SOC pools. Use of leguminous cover-crops produced more SOC (8.7%) than cover-crops without legumes, and further boosted yields of the subsequent corn yields by 34.9% (Joshi, Sieverding et al. 2023). Chapter 4 is the continuation of the first two published meta-analyses (Xu, Sieverding et al., 2019; Joshi, Sieverding et al., 2023).
29: Are agriculture-based emission reductions and offsets permanent? Agricultural products are transitive in nature (e.g., milk spoils, grain molds, oil becomes rancid). Food, feed, fiber, feedstocks, and biofuels have a finite lifespan unless altered or preserved under tightly controlled conditions that curtail microbial or physical decay. Nature and natural processes are cyclic and ever-changing. Yet, the life cycle analytical approach uses permanence as a standard by which agricultural-based emission offsets and reductions are held (Ruseva, Hedrick et al. 2020).

Soil organic carbon (SOC) sequestration in agricultural lands is a GHG offset mechanism in the European Union, Canada, and many other countries, but not within the US. This is in some part, because US farmers and policymakers have past, negative experiences with soil carbon sequestration offsets from the Chicago Climate Exchange (CCX) era. However, the primary reason is a lack of scientific consensus on permanence.

Direct CO2 and GHG removal schemes, and "climate-smart" production practices and products are a popular component of emission reduction targets and strategies (Gardezi, Michael et al. 2021, Abbass, Qasim et al. 2022). The immediate, intermediate, and long-term benefits of specific climate mitigation strategies become uncertain upon close examination. The climate forcing of a GHG is not different because of its source; the atmospheric impact is the same whether CO2 is from a person, plant, biofuel, or fossil fuel. To be successful in stalling or reversing climate change - real, measurable changes in atmospheric composition are needed (IPCC 2023). Soils gain carbon and lose carbon continuously through vegetative growth and decay. For SOC sequestration to be accepted as permanent, the dynamic stability of soil needs to be recognized as a form of

permanence (Dynarski, Bossio et al. 2020). 0: <title>Chapter 4. Meta-Analysis of Soil Carbon Impacts of Manure and Compost Amendments to Corn Production </title>
1: Paired soil organic carbon data on manure and compost amendments to corn (Zea mays) production systems collected from 115 study locations with 375 paired datasets globally were analyzed using meta-analytical methods. Most of the studies used conventional tillage (88%). Corn grain rotations had an average soil carbon increase of 0.4 Mg/ha-yr with organic amendments, however the benefit was greater for corn silage field with a mean soil carbon increase of 1.4 Mg/ha-yr. The benefit of annual manure or compost amendments continued during longer-term experiments, however the average soil organic carbon increases declined over time to 0.2 Mg/ha-yr after 20 years of annual application. On average, soil organic carbon in corn-producing fields with organic amendments increased by an average of 0.8 Mg/ha-yr from 0-15cm, 1.1 Mg/ha-yr from 0-30cm, 1.3 Mg/ha-yr from 0-60cm, and 0.6 Mg/ha-yr from 0-100cm.
2: Livestock and plant wastes or residues have been used throughout the history of mankind to support crop production. These natural byproducts are a key part of ecosystem nutrient cycles and often take the form of manures or composts. Globally, the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) estimates that in 2019 approximately 27 million metric tons of manure was applied to soils (FAO 2023). Manure was estimated to provide approximately 128 million metric tons of nitrogen (N) in 2019 and has been increasing at a rate of about 1 million metric tons per year (FAO

2023). Manures and composts are critical sources of fertilizer for subsistence farmers (38% of N is derived from manure in developing nations) and are viewed as a natural alternative to chemical or mineral nutrient sources now commonly used in industrial agriculture (globally, 25% of N is derived from manure) (FAO 2023). The commercialization of the Haber-Bosch process provided a chemical source of nitrogen (N) which fueled the Green Revolution and modern high-yield crop production systems. This energy and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions intensive process (>2 kgCO2-eq/kg NH3 and 30 GJ/Mg NH3) increases the GHG intensity of crop production and accounts for approximately 2% of global fossil energy use (Liu, Elgowainy et al. 2020, Ghavam, Vahdati et al. 2021). Many green alternatives for the Haber-Bosch process are being explored such as using water electrolysis powered by solar energy, however, most have not achieved large-scale production and present impact trade-offs such as water or rare earth metal use (Smith, Hill et al. 2020). Manure and composts are bio-based N alternatives, but they can generate both benefits (nutrients, microbiota, organic carbon, etc.) and burdens (GHG emissions, water pollution, acidification, etc.) for agricultural producers. The long-term effects of manure and compost use on soil organic carbon (SOC) pools and role in soil carbon sequestration has been debated (Lucas, Holtman et al. 1977, Magdoff 2021). Countless field experiments have been conducted on manure amendments to crop production systems and their soil fertility and carbon effects. Observational evidence has led to two primary schools of thought, 1) most carbon added by manure/compost decomposes over time as soil re-equilibrates back to original conditions (equilibrium model), or 2) the addition of manure/compost can continue to create a new, higher SOC equilibrium level that fluctuates based on climatic, eco-

environmental, and management changes (dynamic model). The permanence manure- and compost-derived SOC has on the production of climate-smart commodities and their GHG accounting is uncertain. To address this issue, a meta-analysis of long-term studies on manure and compost amendment to corn production systems was undertaken.

Manures and composts are organic amendments that are valued in farming systems for their ability to not only provide plant available nutrients (N, P, K, S) but also for their ability to add trace minerals and organic carbon to soils. The sources of organic amendments can be from any livestock (e.g. dairy or beef cattle, swine, broiler or layer poultry, horse, mixed farmyards), with or without bedding and/or residues, and with varying levels of decomposition (composting). The benefits of organic amendments are often distributed over several years. The initial nutrient levels within organic amendments and timing of the nutrient release can differ greatly depending on the source, age, level of processing, handling, climate, seasonality, and application frequency, rate, and form. For example, the C:N ratio of solid beef cattle (Bos taurus) manure averages 14:1 but can range from 6:1 to 28:1 (Manitoba AFRD 2015). Organic amendments can be in liquid, semi-solid, or solid form. They can be surface applied, injected, or incorporated. Organic amendments can be used in cooperation with conservation practices such as reducing tillage, cover cropping, integrating livestock, or diversifying production. The application rate, form, and methods affect the life cycle impacts of the amendment and plant nutrient availability. The inherent heterogeneity of organic amendments makes systematic analysis of their effect challenging.
Meta-analyses are most effective when similar systems are analyzed. To focus the meta-analysis on manure or compost amendment impacts, only soil organic carbon

studies with corn (Zea mays) as a primary rotational crop were included. Corn is globally grown as commodity crop across a wide range of climates (Erenstein, Jaleta et al. 2022). In 2021, corn was produced on 205 Mha with an annual global production of over 1.2 billion Mg (FAO 2023). Corn is annually produced on approximately 12-13% of cropland across the world (FAO 2023). It is a crop that is used as food and livestock feed as well as a biofuel and advanced bioproduct, such as bioplastics, feedstock.

In contrast to the many advantages that manure and compost can provide, organic amendments can also be detrimental to ecosystems and the environment. Improper amendment concentrations, quantities, sourcing, timing, and application methods can result in nutrient loss via greenhouse gas emissions (CH4, N2O) and/or aquatic nitrate and/or phosphate pollution, soil acidification, heavy metal contamination, and/or release of contaminants of emerging concern such as hormones, antibiotics, or endocrine disruptors (Rashmi, Roy et al. 2020). Feedlot or confined animal operations (CAFO) have manure management plans which frequently use cropland application as a waste management method (NRCS 2003). The water content of manure makes it heavy and costly to transport manure and compost. As a result, it is often inefficient from a cost and sustainability perspective to transport large quantities more than 10-20 km from its origin (Keplinger and Hauck 2006, Hadrich, Harrigan et al. 2010). As a result, manure, and to a lesser extent, compost is often locally produced and used based on locally available resources. The geographic variability of manure and compost resources can be significant (Spiegal, Kleinman et al. 2020).
Organic amendments are a natural time-release fertilizer if properly handled but can be a source of pollution if improperly handled (Aillery, Gollehon et al. 2005). Some

of the nutrients within manure or compost are available to plants or microbial organisms at the time of application, while the remainder of the nutrients are bound within plant organic matter. There is often a rapid turnover or movement of labile organic matter fractions which includes dissolved organic carbon (DOC), microbial biomass, easily oxidizable carbon, or particulate organic carbon. The longer-term conversion of plant and organic matter into more stable or nonlabile forms such as humus can release stored nutrients over time. Oxygen availability affects microbial communities (anaerobic VS. aerobic), decomposition rates, and release of nutrients. Decomposition of manure occurs most faster under wet and warm conditions that coincide with periods of greater plant growth. Proper amendment handing, timing, climatic conditions, placement, and application methods are necessary to ensure that nutrients are transferred to crops or held in soils for later plant uptake. Improper handling and application practices can cause aquatic (e.g. NO3, NO2 NH3, P, DOC) and/or atmospheric (e.g. N2O, NH3, CO2, CH4) loss of nutrients or emission of pathogens (e.g. fecal coliform, salmonella) present within manure or compost (Meyer and Mullinax 1999, Smith, Owens et al. 2007, Millner 2009).

For example, the C:N ratio of solid beef cattle (Bos taurus) manure averages 14:1 but can range from 6:1 to 28:1 (Manitoba AFRD 2015). Organic amendments can be in liquid, semi-solid, or solid form. They can be surface applied, injected, or incorporated. Organic amendments can be used in cooperation with conservation practices such as reducing tillage, cover cropping, integrating livestock, or diversifying production. The application rate, form, and methods affect the life cycle impacts of the amendment and plant nutrient availability. The inherent heterogeneity of organic amendments makes systematic analysis of their effect challenging.
Meta-analyses are most effective when similar systems are analyzed. To focus the meta-analysis on manure or compost amendment impacts, only soil organic carbon

studies with corn (Zea mays) as a primary rotational crop were included. Corn is globally grown as commodity crop across a wide range of climates (Erenstein, Jaleta et al. 2022). In 2021, corn was produced on 205 Mha with an annual global production of over 1.2 billion Mg (FAO 2023). Corn is annually produced on approximately 12-13% of cropland across the world (FAO 2023). It is a crop that is used as food and livestock feed as well as a biofuel and advanced bioproduct, such as bioplastics, feedstock.

In contrast to the many advantages that manure and compost can provide, organic amendments can also be detrimental to ecosystems and the environment. Improper amendment concentrations, quantities, sourcing, timing, and application methods can result in nutrient loss via greenhouse gas emissions (CH4, N2O) and/or aquatic nitrate and/or phosphate pollution, soil acidification, heavy metal contamination, and/or release of contaminants of emerging concern such as hormones, antibiotics, or endocrine disruptors (Rashmi, Roy et al. 2020). Feedlot or confined animal operations (CAFO) have manure management plans which frequently use cropland application as a waste management method (NRCS 2003). The water content of manure makes it heavy and costly to transport manure and compost. As a result, it is often inefficient from a cost and sustainability perspective to transport large quantities more than 10-20 km from its origin (Keplinger and Hauck 2006, Hadrich, Harrigan et al. 2010). As a result, manure, and to a lesser extent, compost is often locally produced and used based on locally available resources. The geographic variability of manure and compost resources can be significant (Spiegal, Kleinman et al. 2020).
Organic amendments are a natural time-release fertilizer if properly handled but can be a source of pollution if improperly handled (Aillery, Gollehon et al. 2005). Some

of the nutrients within manure or compost are available to plants or microbial organisms at the time of application, while the remainder of the nutrients are bound within plant organic matter. There is often a rapid turnover or movement of labile organic matter fractions which includes dissolved organic carbon (DOC), microbial biomass, easily oxidizable carbon, or particulate organic carbon. The longer-term conversion of plant and organic matter into more stable or nonlabile forms such as humus can release stored nutrients over time. Oxygen availability affects microbial communities (anaerobic VS. aerobic), decomposition rates, and release of nutrients. Decomposition of manure occurs most faster under wet and warm conditions that coincide with periods of greater plant growth. Proper amendment handing, timing, climatic conditions, placement, and application methods are necessary to ensure that nutrients are transferred to crops or held in soils for later plant uptake. Improper handling and application practices can cause aquatic (e.g. NO3, NO2 NH3, P, DOC) and/or atmospheric (e.g. N2O, NH3, CO2, CH4) loss of nutrients or emission of pathogens (e.g. fecal coliform, salmonella) present within manure or compost (Meyer and Mullinax 1999, Smith, Owens et al. 2007, Millner 2009).

These natural byproducts are a key part of ecosystem nutrient cycles and often take the form of manures or composts. Manure was estimated to provide approximately 128 million metric tons of nitrogen (N) in 2019 and has been increasing at a rate of about 1 million metric tons per year (FAO

2023). Manures and composts are critical sources of fertilizer for subsistence farmers (38% of N is derived from manure in developing nations) and are viewed as a natural alternative to chemical or mineral nutrient sources now commonly used in industrial agriculture (globally, 25% of N is derived from manure) (FAO 2023). The commercialization of the Haber-Bosch process provided a chemical source of nitrogen (N) which fueled the Green Revolution and modern high-yield crop production systems. This energy and greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions intensive process (>2 kgCO2-eq/kg NH3 and 30 GJ/Mg NH3) increases the GHG intensity of crop production and accounts for approximately 2% of global fossil energy use (Liu, Elgowainy et al. 2020, Ghavam, Vahdati et al. 2021). Many green alternatives for the Haber-Bosch process are being explored such as using water electrolysis powered by solar energy, however, most have not achieved large-scale production and present impact trade-offs such as water or rare earth metal use (Smith, Hill et al. 2020). Manure and composts are bio-based N alternatives, but they can generate both benefits (nutrients, microbiota, organic carbon, etc.) and burdens (GHG emissions, water pollution, acidification, etc.) for agricultural producers. The long-term effects of manure and compost use on soil organic carbon (SOC) pools and role in soil carbon sequestration has been debated (Lucas, Holtman et al. 1977, Magdoff 2021). Countless field experiments have been conducted on manure amendments to crop production systems and their soil fertility and carbon effects. Observational evidence has led to two primary schools of thought, 1) most carbon added by manure/compost decomposes over time as soil re-equilibrates back to original conditions (equilibrium model), or 2) the addition of manure/compost can continue to create a new, higher SOC equilibrium level that fluctuates based on climatic, eco-

environmental, and management changes (dynamic model). The permanence manure- and compost-derived SOC has on the production of climate-smart commodities and their GHG accounting is uncertain. To address this issue, a meta-analysis of long-term studies on manure and compost amendment to corn production systems was undertaken.

Many green alternatives for the Haber-Bosch process are being explored such as using water electrolysis powered by solar energy, however, most have not achieved large-scale production and present impact trade-offs such as water or rare earth metal use (Smith, Hill et al. 2020). Manure and composts are bio-based N alternatives, but they can generate both benefits (nutrients, microbiota, organic carbon, etc.) and burdens (GHG emissions, water pollution, acidification, etc.) for agricultural producers. The long-term effects of manure and compost use on soil organic carbon (SOC) pools and role in soil carbon sequestration has been debated (Lucas, Holtman et al. 1977, Magdoff 2021). Countless field experiments have been conducted on manure amendments to crop production systems and their soil fertility and carbon effects. Observational evidence has led to two primary schools of thought, 1) most carbon added by manure/compost decomposes over time as soil re-equilibrates back to original conditions (equilibrium model), or 2) the addition of manure/compost can continue to create a new, higher SOC equilibrium level that fluctuates based on climatic, eco-

environmental, and management changes (dynamic model). The permanence manure- and compost-derived SOC has on the production of climate-smart commodities and their GHG accounting is uncertain. To address this issue, a meta-analysis of long-term studies on manure and compost amendment to corn production systems was undertaken.

Manures and composts are organic amendments that are valued in farming systems for their ability to not only provide plant available nutrients (N, P, K, S) but also for their ability to add trace minerals and organic carbon to soils. The sources of organic amendments can be from any livestock (e.g. dairy or beef cattle, swine, broiler or layer poultry, horse, mixed farmyards), with or without bedding and/or residues, and with varying levels of decomposition (composting). The benefits of organic amendments are often distributed over several years. The initial nutrient levels within organic amendments and timing of the nutrient release can differ greatly depending on the source, age, level of processing, handling, climate, seasonality, and application frequency, rate, and form. For example, the C:N ratio of solid beef cattle (Bos taurus) manure averages 14:1 but can range from 6:1 to 28:1 (Manitoba AFRD 2015). Organic amendments can be in liquid, semi-solid, or solid form. They can be surface applied, injected, or incorporated. Organic amendments can be used in cooperation with conservation practices such as reducing tillage, cover cropping, integrating livestock, or diversifying production. The application rate, form, and methods affect the life cycle impacts of the amendment and plant nutrient availability. The inherent heterogeneity of organic amendments makes systematic analysis of their effect challenging.
Meta-analyses are most effective when similar systems are analyzed. To focus the meta-analysis on manure or compost amendment impacts, only soil organic carbon

studies with corn (Zea mays) as a primary rotational crop were included. Corn is globally grown as commodity crop across a wide range of climates (Erenstein, Jaleta et al. 2022). In 2021, corn was produced on 205 Mha with an annual global production of over 1.2 billion Mg (FAO 2023). Corn is annually produced on approximately 12-13% of cropland across the world (FAO 2023). It is a crop that is used as food and livestock feed as well as a biofuel and advanced bioproduct, such as bioplastics, feedstock.

In contrast to the many advantages that manure and compost can provide, organic amendments can also be detrimental to ecosystems and the environment. Improper amendment concentrations, quantities, sourcing, timing, and application methods can result in nutrient loss via greenhouse gas emissions (CH4, N2O) and/or aquatic nitrate and/or phosphate pollution, soil acidification, heavy metal contamination, and/or release of contaminants of emerging concern such as hormones, antibiotics, or endocrine disruptors (Rashmi, Roy et al. 2020). Feedlot or confined animal operations (CAFO) have manure management plans which frequently use cropland application as a waste management method (NRCS 2003). The water content of manure makes it heavy and costly to transport manure and compost. As a result, it is often inefficient from a cost and sustainability perspective to transport large quantities more than 10-20 km from its origin (Keplinger and Hauck 2006, Hadrich, Harrigan et al. 2010). As a result, manure, and to a lesser extent, compost is often locally produced and used based on locally available resources. The geographic variability of manure and compost resources can be significant (Spiegal, Kleinman et al. 2020).
Organic amendments are a natural time-release fertilizer if properly handled but can be a source of pollution if improperly handled (Aillery, Gollehon et al. 2005). Some

of the nutrients within manure or compost are available to plants or microbial organisms at the time of application, while the remainder of the nutrients are bound within plant organic matter. There is often a rapid turnover or movement of labile organic matter fractions which includes dissolved organic carbon (DOC), microbial biomass, easily oxidizable carbon, or particulate organic carbon. The longer-term conversion of plant and organic matter into more stable or nonlabile forms such as humus can release stored nutrients over time. Oxygen availability affects microbial communities (anaerobic VS. aerobic), decomposition rates, and release of nutrients. Decomposition of manure occurs most faster under wet and warm conditions that coincide with periods of greater plant growth. Proper amendment handing, timing, climatic conditions, placement, and application methods are necessary to ensure that nutrients are transferred to crops or held in soils for later plant uptake. Improper handling and application practices can cause aquatic (e.g. NO3, NO2 NH3, P, DOC) and/or atmospheric (e.g. N2O, NH3, CO2, CH4) loss of nutrients or emission of pathogens (e.g. fecal coliform, salmonella) present within manure or compost (Meyer and Mullinax 1999, Smith, Owens et al. 2007, Millner 2009).
10: <header>4.3 Methods </header>

Data were collected from peer-reviewed publications on the soil organic carbon effects of compost- or manure-based organic amendments on corn production systems. Data collection and analytical methods closely followed Xu, Sieverding et al., 2019. The database was developed following these steps: 1) keyword literature search, 2) screening publications for relevancy, 3) extracting data, 4) assessing data quality, and 5) performing statistical analyses.

Peer reviewed publications on SOC changes for corn-based rotations were identified using Clarivate's Web of Science (https://www.webofscience.com) Advanced Search. Publications in English were selected using the key search terms of TS= ((corn OR maize OR "zea mays") AND (soil carbon OR "soil C" OR "organic carbon" OR "organic C" OR "soil organic matter" or "carbon sequestration" OR "soil health" OR "soil quality") AND (residue* OR stover OR straw OR tillage OR manure* OR irrigat* OR cover crop OR "cover crops" OR "organic amendment" OR "green manure" OR "green manures" OR "cover-crop" OR compost* OR "dung" OR "organic fertilizer" OR "organic fertilizers" OR "guano" OR biosolid* OR "organic waste" OR "organic wastes" OR "animal waste" OR "animal wastes")). The keyword search resulted in a list of 7226 peer-reviewed papers within the Clarivate database published between 1969 and June 2021. These papers were cross-referenced with previous meta-analyses using Microsoft Excel to reduce the number of papers to 3737 unevaluated papers (cite 2019 meta- analysis). Papers relevant to cover crops were evaluated as part of a companion study (Joshi et al. 2023).
Abstracts were used to identify papers that contained SOC data resulting from compost and manure addition, paper were evaluated based on the following screening criteria (Figure A-1):

<list>Corn was the primary rotational cash crop. 
Measurements or experiments were three years in duration or longer. 
The study was based on field experiments. Greenhouse, laboratory, simulation, and modeling studies without a field experimental component were excluded. 
Soil organic carbon was measured.</list>

<list>Defined manure, compost, or some combination of these organic amendments were included in the experiment. 
The experimental design included both a control (no organic amendment) and treatment (organic amendment) groups.</list>

The screening identified 277 papers as likely containing relevant information (Figure A-1). These papers were read and manually analyzed. This resulted in 86 papers being selected for use in the meta-analysis (Table A-1). From these papers, 375 replicated treatment-pairs were extracted. Study duration varied, however, a large proportion (54%) papers were on experiments had a duration of greater than 10 years (Figure 4.1). As a result, many had many publications referencing the same experiment. A total of 64 papers were identified which contained data on the same experiment (Table A- 1). Data from experiments reported in multiple studies were combined and duplicates were removed.
Duration of Studies (Years)
Figure 4.1. Bar chart showing the study duration for the paired datapoints included in the analysis.

Pairs from 115 study locations were selected to isolate the effect of manure or compost addition to the greatest extent possible. Experiments with multiple manure or compost types and application rates were paired with the same control or check data. Data presented in the form of graphs were extracted using Web Plot Digitizer (https://apps.automeris.io/wpd). If management practice, climate, or soil property data were not available within the publication, data was collected from other literature describing the same experiment. If experiment-specific data was unavailable, temporal and spatial data specific to the region were used. For example, average soil bulk density and climatic data were populated by local data such as from SSURGO and agACIS and then by global (soilgrids and NOAA Climate Data Online climate normals) spatial databases in the event more specific data were unavailable. Controls with and without chemical fertilization were included. Quantitative (i.e. soil bulk density, soil texture, climate, corn yield, measurement variability, replicates, rotation, harvest type and residue removal, tillage, manure or compost properties and use, irrigation, single or double cropping practices, chemical fertilization) and qualitative (i.e. control type, pedotransfer, assumptions, and confidence) data on the replicated pairs were tracked. Data on manure properties and application rates were standardized to an annualized average. Data from studies using human-derived biosolids were collected but excluded from the analysis. Unfortunately, initial SOC measurements were often only reported at the end of the study, SO evaluation of the temporal effect was limited (Figure 4.2).
Duration of Studies (Years)
Figure 4.2. Bar chart showing the number of paired datasets and their duration. The mean duration of studies included in the analysis was 16 years.

Data on manure or compost dry mass, nutrient composition (N, P, and C), application method, and other properties were aggregated from original or companion literature. Where nutrient composition was not available, average regional values based on animal type and handling practices were used. Multiple data sources were used to gather enough information to conduct meaningful analyses. If manure or compost properties were unavailable, they were assumed based on other regionally reported values based on source and experimental location. If soil properties were not described in the publication or companion papers, soil texture data was extracted from national databases such as the Web Soil Survey or from Soilgrids.org based on location. Depths were standardized to 0-15, 0-30, 0-60, and 0-100 cm, accounting for respectively 52%, 30%, 12%, and 6% of the paired datasets. Tilled soils were assumed to have well mixed SOC. Untilled soils (no-till) were assumed to have SOC stratification based on regional no-till agricultural soil profiles. No-till soils were assumed have a greater percentage of SOC distributed near the surface (Blanco-Canqui, Shapiro et al. 2021).

Experimental pairs were carefully selected SO that the effect of the manure or compost addition could be isolated. Most of the experimental pairs had a control treatment which was fertilized (58%), the remainder (42%) had an unfertilized control. Control treatments that were fertilized had a 17% higher yield than unfertilized. Fertilization levels commonly matched the nutrients provided by the organic amendment, only from chemical sources. The average number of replicates across all experiments was 3.3 with an average of 6.5 soil samples were composited for each replicate. Most of the experiments were conducted in Asia or North America (67%) with a total of 115 locations included from across the world (Figure 4.3).

Figure 4.3. World map showing the updated Köppen-Geiger classification color code (Red-pink tones as tropical (A), yellow-brown tones as dry (B), green tones as temperate (C), and purple-blue tones as continental (D)) with black dots representing the location of paired datasets (Ellerstrom, Strehl et al. 2006). China and the United States are outlined in gray and were respectively the sources of 45% and 22% of the paired datasets.

14: Data were collected from peer-reviewed publications on the soil organic carbon effects of compost- or manure-based organic amendments on corn production systems. Data collection and analytical methods closely followed Xu, Sieverding et al., 2019. The database was developed following these steps: 1) keyword literature search, 2) screening publications for relevancy, 3) extracting data, 4) assessing data quality, and 5) performing statistical analyses.

Peer reviewed publications on SOC changes for corn-based rotations were identified using Clarivate's Web of Science (https://www.webofscience.com) Advanced Search. Publications in English were selected using the key search terms of TS= ((corn OR maize OR "zea mays") AND (soil carbon OR "soil C" OR "organic carbon" OR "organic C" OR "soil organic matter" or "carbon sequestration" OR "soil health" OR "soil quality") AND (residue* OR stover OR straw OR tillage OR manure* OR irrigat* OR cover crop OR "cover crops" OR "organic amendment" OR "green manure" OR "green manures" OR "cover-crop" OR compost* OR "dung" OR "organic fertilizer" OR "organic fertilizers" OR "guano" OR biosolid* OR "organic waste" OR "organic wastes" OR "animal waste" OR "animal wastes")). The keyword search resulted in a list of 7226 peer-reviewed papers within the Clarivate database published between 1969 and June 2021. These papers were cross-referenced with previous meta-analyses using Microsoft Excel to reduce the number of papers to 3737 unevaluated papers (cite 2019 meta- analysis). Papers relevant to cover crops were evaluated as part of a companion study (Joshi et al. 2023).
Abstracts were used to identify papers that contained SOC data resulting from compost and manure addition, paper were evaluated based on the following screening criteria (Figure A-1):

<list>Corn was the primary rotational cash crop. 
Measurements or experiments were three years in duration or longer. 
The study was based on field experiments. Greenhouse, laboratory, simulation, and modeling studies without a field experimental component were excluded. 
Soil organic carbon was measured.</list>

<list>Defined manure, compost, or some combination of these organic amendments were included in the experiment. 
The experimental design included both a control (no organic amendment) and treatment (organic amendment) groups.</list>

The screening identified 277 papers as likely containing relevant information (Figure A-1). These papers were read and manually analyzed. This resulted in 86 papers being selected for use in the meta-analysis (Table A-1). From these papers, 375 replicated treatment-pairs were extracted. Study duration varied, however, a large proportion (54%) papers were on experiments had a duration of greater than 10 years (Figure 4.1). As a result, many had many publications referencing the same experiment. A total of 64 papers were identified which contained data on the same experiment (Table A- 1). Data from experiments reported in multiple studies were combined and duplicates were removed.
Figure 4.1. Bar chart showing the study duration for the paired datapoints included in the analysis.

Pairs from 115 study locations were selected to isolate the effect of manure or compost addition to the greatest extent possible. Experiments with multiple manure or compost types and application rates were paired with the same control or check data. Data presented in the form of graphs were extracted using Web Plot Digitizer (https://apps.automeris.io/wpd). If management practice, climate, or soil property data were not available within the publication, data was collected from other literature describing the same experiment. If experiment-specific data was unavailable, temporal and spatial data specific to the region were used. For example, average soil bulk density and climatic data were populated by local data such as from SSURGO and agACIS and then by global (soilgrids and NOAA Climate Data Online climate normals) spatial databases in the event more specific data were unavailable. Controls with and without chemical fertilization were included. Quantitative (i.e. soil bulk density, soil texture, climate, corn yield, measurement variability, replicates, rotation, harvest type and residue removal, tillage, manure or compost properties and use, irrigation, single or double cropping practices, chemical fertilization) and qualitative (i.e. control type, pedotransfer, assumptions, and confidence) data on the replicated pairs were tracked. Data on manure properties and application rates were standardized to an annualized average. Data from studies using human-derived biosolids were collected but excluded from the analysis. Unfortunately, initial SOC measurements were often only reported at the end of the study, SO evaluation of the temporal effect was limited (Figure 4.2).
Figure 4.2. Bar chart showing the number of paired datasets and their duration. The mean duration of studies included in the analysis was 16 years.

Data on manure or compost dry mass, nutrient composition (N, P, and C), application method, and other properties were aggregated from original or companion literature. Where nutrient composition was not available, average regional values based on animal type and handling practices were used. Multiple data sources were used to gather enough information to conduct meaningful analyses. If manure or compost properties were unavailable, they were assumed based on other regionally reported values based on source and experimental location. If soil properties were not described in the publication or companion papers, soil texture data was extracted from national databases such as the Web Soil Survey or from Soilgrids.org based on location. Depths were standardized to 0-15, 0-30, 0-60, and 0-100 cm, accounting for respectively 52%, 30%, 12%, and 6% of the paired datasets. Tilled soils were assumed to have well mixed SOC. Untilled soils (no-till) were assumed to have SOC stratification based on regional no-till agricultural soil profiles. No-till soils were assumed have a greater percentage of SOC distributed near the surface (Blanco-Canqui, Shapiro et al. 2021).

Experimental pairs were carefully selected SO that the effect of the manure or compost addition could be isolated. Most of the experimental pairs had a control treatment which was fertilized (58%), the remainder (42%) had an unfertilized control. Control treatments that were fertilized had a 17% higher yield than unfertilized. Fertilization levels commonly matched the nutrients provided by the organic amendment, only from chemical sources. The average number of replicates across all experiments was 3.3 with an average of 6.5 soil samples were composited for each replicate. Most of the experiments were conducted in Asia or North America (67%) with a total of 115 locations included from across the world (Figure 4.3).

Figure 4.3. World map showing the updated Köppen-Geiger classification color code (Red-pink tones as tropical (A), yellow-brown tones as dry (B), green tones as temperate (C), and purple-blue tones as continental (D)) with black dots representing the location of paired datasets (Ellerstrom, Strehl et al. 2006). China and the United States are outlined in gray and were respectively the sources of 45% and 22% of the paired datasets.

18: Most of the site locations were for temperate (51%) or continental (35%) climates with the remainder in regions classified as dry or tropical according to the Köppen- Geiger climate classification (Figures 4.3 and 4.4). The majority of the fields were

dryland (58%) with management practices equally split (50%) between single (e.g. one crop per calendar year) and double (two crops per calendar year) cropping systems (Figure 4.5). Regions with less than 600 mm per year of precipitation require irrigation to grow corn and regions with a mean temperature of 12-13°C have the potential for year- round crop production (Ramirez-Cabral, Kumar et al. 2017, Prasad, Gunn et al. 2018). Areas with double or triple cropping typically used corn as the warm-season (summer) crop.
Figure 4.4. Bar chart showing number of paired datasets included per climate type (Ellerstrom, Strehl et al. 2006).

Figure 4.5. Scatterplot comparing the growing season (green triangle) and annual mean (black circle) precipitation (y-axis) and temperature (x-axis) with respect to cropping system type (shown as pie charts) for the study locations. Dashed lines indicate the typical climatic thresholds for corn.

Soil organic carbon measurements were converted to a standardized unit (Eq. 4.1 or 4.2). Soil organic carbon estimates were corrected to reflect soil equivalent mass (SEM) where adequate information was available (Eq. 4.3). Fifty six percent of paired datasets did not have the soil density change needed to perform SEM corrections. Most of the experimental sites (77%) had medium or medium-fine textured soils (Figure 4.6).

21: Half of the paired datasets were within regions which produced one crop per year, the remainder were within climates capable of producing two or more crops per field per year (Figure 4.4). For areas that practiced double or triple cropping, only the management practices and amendments used to produce corn were evaluated. Irrigation was used in 42% of the paired datasets. The majority (88%) of paired datasets were from fields managed using conventional tillage. Conventional tillage was defined based on implement type. Implements included as conventional tillage were moldboard plow, chisel plow, disk plow, and rotary-till. Experiments where amendment was described as incorporated were defined as conventional tillage. Few (12%) of paired datasets were from fields managed using no-till or reduced-till practices. Field experiments which used no-till or reduced-till practices frequently incorporated cover cropping into their management practices. Data collected on studies using cover-cropping were evaluated by

Joshi et al. (2023). Few The corn-based crop rotations typically consisted of two crops within the rotation (44%), most commonly corn-wheat or corn-soybean. The second most common rotational practice was continuous corn (41%) with the remainder ranging from three to five crop rotations with corn as the primary cash crop. Most experiments (89%) grew corn for grain production, the remainder were harvested fully or in part as silage. The average treatment corn yield was 8.8+3.3 Mg/ha and control yield was 6.9+3.1 Mg/ha. For silage crops, a harvest index of 0.5 on a dry matter basis was applied to the total above ground biomass yield where available to estimate grain production. Unfertilized checks had approximately 17% lower yields than fertilized checks.

Joshi et al. (2023). Few The corn-based crop rotations typically consisted of two crops within the rotation (44%), most commonly corn-wheat or corn-soybean. The second most common rotational practice was continuous corn (41%) with the remainder ranging from three to five crop rotations with corn as the primary cash crop. 23: <header>4.3.3. Manure Properties </header>

The majority (99%) of the paired datasets were for manure-based organic amendments that contained (36%) or did not contain (64%) undigested plant residue and/or bedding. Few (16%) of the organic amendments had been composted. The organic amendment moisture content averaged 54% with the majority (76%) solid in form. The average organic amendment application rate was on a dry mass basis 14=12.2 Mg/ha-yr. Manure was predominantly incorporated or injected (73%) with the remainder mostly top-dressed or broadcasted (26%) over the soil surface. Over half of paired datasets used cattle (42%) or swine (20%) manure. The remainder used mixed (28%) sources or individual livestock (e.g. horse, poultry, sheep) or plant-based compost (e.g. orchard trimmings, food waste, crop residue, oilseed meal, etc.) sources (10%). The average estimated nutrient content of the organic amendment by weight was 25% carbon, 2.1% nitrogen, and 0.8% phosphorous. On average, organic amendments within the

paired dataset annually provided 3.1 Mg C/ha, 170 kg N/ha, and 73 kg P/ha to corn production (Figure 4.7). In addition to the organic amendments, 38% of the paired datasets had supplemental synthetic N added annually at an average rate of 120 kg N/ha.

25: <header>4.3.4. Meta-analysis Methods </header>

Agronomic field test results are affected by many location-specific variables, such as weather, prior land use, soil type, and crop production practices; to address these site- specific issues in a quantitative manner, meta-analytic methods were used following Xu, Sieverding et al. (2019) and Joshi, Sieverding et al. (2023). Individual studies can yield conflicting results. To identify patterns not discernable in individual studies while considering site-specific variables, the SOC pool size from the experimental treatment was evaluated with respect to a control or check treatment that was conducted at the same location and during the same time frame. The mean results of the experimental treatments (SOCtr) were compared to the check (SOCck) using a logarithmic response ratio (RR).
RR = Ln (SoCu) Where: RR = Response ratio (unitless)
SOCtr = Soil Organic Carbon with Organic Amendment Treatment (Maa) SOCck = Soil Organic Carbon of Untreated Check/Control (Maa)

The SOC change (ASOC) between corn production systems with (SOCtr) and without (SOCck) organic amendments was calculated using the result at the end of the field experiment (Figure 4.10). The check or control treatment is used as a site-specific baseline to determine if the amendment increases SOC. Differences in the annualized

rate of change (ASOCr) was calculated where data was available on fields for both the start and end of the experiment (Eq. 4.7). The RR and ASOCr are both useful in evaluating the effectiveness of amendments in improving SOC sequestration.

27: Most studies found that SOC was generally increased by organic amendments across all soil depths (Figure 4.8), although the relative rate of change decreased slightly

with depth (Figure 4.9). This result is consistent with SOC models such as Century or DNDC (Li, Frolking et al. 1994, Li, Wen et al. 2018). On average, the soils treated with organic amendments were observed to have a 0-15cm SOC sequestration rate of 0.8+1.3 Mg C/ha-yr greater than that of the check.

Figure 4.9. Bubble diagram of all paired datasets based on duration of study and depth.

29: The greatest number of experimental results were obtained for SOC dynamics within a depth range of 0-15cm. Smaller soil particles (clays, silts) bind with SOC due to surficial electrical charge. It is generally assumed that soils higher in clay will retain SOC better. Looking at the effects of texture on the SOC rate of change, medium and fine soils had a lower rate of change (Figure 4.10), however, medium-fine soils had a much greater variability and rate of change. The number of samples for each soil texture varies substantially. The average bulk density of treated soils from 0-15cm decreased over the duration of the studies due to organic amendments from 1.32+0.16 to 1.28+0.14 g/cm³ while the control treatments (1.33+0.15g/cm3) remained similar to pre-treatment bulk densities.
Figure 4.10. Box whisker plot representing the percent soil organic carbon change rate (ASOCr) of paired datasets which had data for 0-15cm separated based on soil texture.

The average rate of change from manure or compost amendment decreases slightly over time (Figure 4.11) and decreases with depth and tillage. Soils from 0-15cm without density corrections had a SOC sequestration rate of 0.9+1.5 Mg C/ha-yr whereas

those with density corrections had a rate of 0.30.3 Mg C/ha-yr greater for the treatment than for the check. The SOC pool values after adjustment for plot starting differences after treatment with organic amendments averaged 33.3+20.3 Mg C/ha for 0-15cm, 55.3+27.8 Mg C/ha for 0-30cm, 93.7+49.0 Mg C/ha for 0-60cm, and 97.8+43.3 Mg C/ha for 0-100cm at the end of the studies. Surficial soil layers were more greatly affected by organic amendment. The relative response ratios were 0.3+0.3 for 0-15cm, 0.2+0.2 for 0- 30cm, 0.1+0.1 for 0-60cm, and 0.1+0.2 for 0-100cm. Conventionally tilled systems yielded lower average SOC sequestration rates (0.7+1.3 Mg C/ha-yr from 0-15cm and 1.0+2.0 Mg C/ha-yr from 0-30cm) than no-till systems (1.1+1.21 Mg C/ha-yr from 0-15cm and 1.1+1.5 Mg C/ha-yr from 0-30cm).
Figure 4.11. Box whisker plot of soil organic carbon change rate (ASOCr) for 0-15cm depth based on duration of study.
Other variables, such as climate and quantity of the manure or compost amendment had an inconsistent impact on ASOCr. Climate had a variable effect on the

SOC change rate, with Köppen-Geiger climate zones with temperate or continental climate warm summers and no dry season (Figure 4.12) having the greatest variability. However, these climate zones also had fewer paired datasets, which increases the uncertainty of the statistical analysis. The average ASOC1 generally increased by 0.1% per Mg C/ha-yr added through organic amendments until greater the 4 Mg C/ha-yr was added to the system (Figure 4.13). An amendment adding > 4 Mg C/ha-yr translates to a manure or compost amendment of approximately 16 Mg DM/ha-yr with a nutrient value of 330 kg N/ha-yr and 120 kg P/ha-yr.
Figure 4.12. Box whisker plot of soil organic carbon change rate (ASOCr) for 0-15cm depth based on the most common Köppen-Geiger classifications.
1-2 Mg C/ha-yr 2-3 Mg C/ha-yr 3-4 Mg C/ha-yr > 4 Mg C/ha-yr

35: Most of the papers required assumptions to enable a comparative analysis (see Appendix B spreadsheets for details). The results were reported in the papers for a range of depths with differing levels of detail regarding soil characteristics. To compensate, pedotransfer techniques were used to infill missing data from spatial scientific databases on soil type characteristics. Only 18% of the paired datasets did not require pedotransfer. Thirty percent (30%) of paired datasets required only depth adjustment (e.g., data was reported for 0-10 cm and had to be adjusted to 0-15 cm) Over half, 52% were missing soil texture or bulk density information (e.g., only one bulk density measurement). Soil organic carbon measurement methods differed substantially; 54% used a variation of Walkley and Black, 27% used a CN or TOC analyzer, 8% used loss on ignition, 4% used a variant of sulfochromic wet digestion, 3% used a spectrophotometer, and the remainder

(4%) used other methods or did not report the analytical method. Soil organic matter (SOM) was reported in 14% of studies and a Van Bemmelen factor (1.724) was applied to convert it to SOC. The assumption that 58% of SOM is SOC is generally viewed as a reasonable estimate, however the C content of SOM has been shown to vary spatially and temporally from 36-98% (Pribyl 2010). The average number of replicates used within studies was between 3 and 4. The quantity of soil subsamples collected per replicate was an average of 6.5. However, despite the overall quality of the experimental design, 47% of paired datasets did not have a statistical assessment (e.g., standard deviation, confidence, etc.) of SOC results or information on replicates reported. Only 1% of paired datasets had all statistical data desired available (i.e., standard deviation of SOC pools, SOC change rate or temporal change, and bulk density). A confidence grade (e.g., A = no assumptions, B = minor assumptions such as yield or amendment composition, C = assumptions for one or two key inputs, or D = substantial assumption, primarily lack of bulk density information) was assigned based on assumptions. Most studies (58%) were rated as 'D', 25% were rated as 'C', and only 19% were rated as 'A' or 'B'.
36: Most of the papers required assumptions to enable a comparative analysis (see Appendix B spreadsheets for details). The results were reported in the papers for a range of depths with differing levels of detail regarding soil characteristics. To compensate, pedotransfer techniques were used to infill missing data from spatial scientific databases on soil type characteristics. Only 18% of the paired datasets did not require pedotransfer. Thirty percent (30%) of paired datasets required only depth adjustment (e.g., data was reported for 0-10 cm and had to be adjusted to 0-15 cm) Over half, 52% were missing soil texture or bulk density information (e.g., only one bulk density measurement). Soil organic carbon measurement methods differed substantially; 54% used a variation of Walkley and Black, 27% used a CN or TOC analyzer, 8% used loss on ignition, 4% used a variant of sulfochromic wet digestion, 3% used a spectrophotometer, and the remainder

(4%) used other methods or did not report the analytical method. Soil organic matter (SOM) was reported in 14% of studies and a Van Bemmelen factor (1.724) was applied to convert it to SOC. The assumption that 58% of SOM is SOC is generally viewed as a reasonable estimate, however the C content of SOM has been shown to vary spatially and temporally from 36-98% (Pribyl 2010). The average number of replicates used within studies was between 3 and 4. The quantity of soil subsamples collected per replicate was an average of 6.5. However, despite the overall quality of the experimental design, 47% of paired datasets did not have a statistical assessment (e.g., standard deviation, confidence, etc.) of SOC results or information on replicates reported. Only 1% of paired datasets had all statistical data desired available (i.e., standard deviation of SOC pools, SOC change rate or temporal change, and bulk density). A confidence grade (e.g., A = no assumptions, B = minor assumptions such as yield or amendment composition, C = assumptions for one or two key inputs, or D = substantial assumption, primarily lack of bulk density information) was assigned based on assumptions. Most studies (58%) were rated as 'D', 25% were rated as 'C', and only 19% were rated as 'A' or 'B'.
37: Most of the field experiments included in this analysis were conventionally managed. Manure and compost amendments were incorporated into the soil for 70% of the paired datasets. Leaving manure or compost amendments on the surface of the soil increases the likelihood of nutrient run-off or denitrification, particularly if moisture-rich amendments are applied to the surface of frozen soils (Mahajan and Varma 2022, Saha, Cibin et al. 2023). Incorporating or injecting organic amendments into the soil places the

nutrients within the amendments near the root zone where they can be more readily accessed by crops. The large proportion of the studies included in this meta-analysis using tillage provides insight into short-term and long-term options for increasing SOC in tilled soils. Tillage is used in modern agricultural practices primarily to control weeds without or in conjunction with the use of herbicides. Many organic crop producers use tillage as a weed control method (Liebert, Benner et al. 2022). As more weeds become herbicide- resistant, use of tillage as a weed control method may increase (Sawadgo and Plastina 2022). The net increases in SOC observed in this meta-analysis supports use of manure or compost amendments as a method to support soil development when tillage is used.

The management practices used within the check or control treatment varied across studies. Efforts were made to create paired datasets that isolated the effect of the organic amendment, however the ASOCr and RR are based on the difference between the treatment and the check, SO the management practices used in the check have a direct effect on the analytical results (Figure 4.14). There were two primary types of checks, fertilized and unfertilized. In most instances, the fertilized checks were designed to provide the crop with same nutrients as the organic amendment, only from synthetic or chemical sources. The unfertilized checks showed a greater relative increase in SOC for shallow (0-15 and 0-30 cm) experimental results with greater variability. The experiments which used deeper soil sampling (0-60 and 0-100 cm) had relatively lower ASOCr with less variability. This pattern may be due to the relatively small number of deep test results available and the influence of soil dynamics occurring deeper within the root zone and the natural decrease in SOC with depth. Within natural soil profiles, more SOC is present near the surface within the O and A horizons. Most tillage implements do

not penetrate greater than 30 cm. As a result, the effect of incorporated, injected, or surface applied organic amendments is primarily near the surface of the soil profile. There is not enough test available for deeper depths to conduct a robust statistical evaluation. Overall, only a fraction (37%) of paired datasets used soil equivalent mass or contained enough information to make a density correction. Where bulk density was reported, there was ~4% decrease in density was present. Bulk density changes affect SOC pool estimations, lack of use of soil equivalent mass magnifies change rates. The percent change rate for 0-15 cm paired datasets without soil equivalent mass corrections was 0.9+1.5% while those with the correction was 0.3+0.3%. The effect of density changes is reduced when the response ratios are used in lieu of change or SOC sequestration rate estimations.

Figure 4.14. Box whisker plot of paired datasets based on depth and fertilization (F) or lack of fertilization (U) or lack of fertilization of the control/check used in the paired dataset. Fifty-eight percent (58%) of paired datasets had fertilized controls.

Organic amendments are most often used by farmers as a source of N, P, and micronutrients, not as a C supplement. Increased SOC supports moisture retention and soil health but it does not typically limit plant growth because plants can obtain the C they need from the air. The quantity of N and P needed for corn production varies based on many factors such as crop rotation, regional soil properties, and yield targets. The organic amendments in many treatments did not provide all the nitrogen needed for corn production (Figure 4.15). Conversely, some of the treatment applied more N or P than what was needed (Figure 4.16). This nutrient surplus does not always increase the risk of nutrient loss, not all nutrients in compost or manure is plant available. Much of the nutrient content is bound within undecayed organic matter that will not be released until it decays. The proportion of undecayed organic matter is larger if the manure is mixed with bedding from livestock, which was a common practice in the included experiments (Figure 4.17).
Synthetic N Use in Treatment in Addition to Organic Amendments
Figure 4.15. Bar chart showing the level of synthetic fertilizer applied in addition to organic amendments in treatments.

Figure 4.16. Bar chart showing the estimated nitrogen (N) value of the organic amendments applied to corn. Note, N value of manure or compost is highly variable. This estimate is derived from nutrient analyses and average test results based on manure or compost source and processing.
Figure 4.17. Bar chart showing the number of paired datasets that were composted and inclusion of bedding or residue in manure amendment.
Quality of experimental data reporting and publications needs to be improved.
Most publications were lacking critical bulk density information over time or did not report adequate data on replicates.

Overall, only a fraction (37%) of paired datasets used soil equivalent mass or contained enough information to make a density correction. Where bulk density was reported, there was ~4% decrease in density was present. Bulk density changes affect SOC pool estimations, lack of use of soil equivalent mass magnifies change rates. The percent change rate for 0-15 cm paired datasets without soil equivalent mass corrections was 0.9+1.5% while those with the correction was 0.3+0.3%. The effect of density changes is reduced when the response ratios are used in lieu of change or SOC sequestration rate estimations.

Figure 4.14. Box whisker plot of paired datasets based on depth and fertilization (F) or lack of fertilization (U) or lack of fertilization of the control/check used in the paired dataset. Fifty-eight percent (58%) of paired datasets had fertilized controls.

Organic amendments are most often used by farmers as a source of N, P, and micronutrients, not as a C supplement. Increased SOC supports moisture retention and soil health but it does not typically limit plant growth because plants can obtain the C they need from the air. The quantity of N and P needed for corn production varies based on many factors such as crop rotation, regional soil properties, and yield targets. The organic amendments in many treatments did not provide all the nitrogen needed for corn production (Figure 4.15). Conversely, some of the treatment applied more N or P than what was needed (Figure 4.16). This nutrient surplus does not always increase the risk of nutrient loss, not all nutrients in compost or manure is plant available. Much of the nutrient content is bound within undecayed organic matter that will not be released until it decays. The proportion of undecayed organic matter is larger if the manure is mixed with bedding from livestock, which was a common practice in the included experiments (Figure 4.17).
Synthetic N Use in Treatment in Addition to Organic Amendments

Figure 4.16. Bar chart showing the estimated nitrogen (N) value of the organic amendments applied to corn. Note, N value of manure or compost is highly variable. This estimate is derived from nutrient analyses and average test results based on manure or compost source and processing.
Figure 4.17. Bar chart showing the number of paired datasets that were composted and inclusion of bedding or residue in manure amendment.
Quality of experimental data reporting and publications needs to be improved.
Most publications were lacking critical bulk density information over time or did not report adequate data on replicates.
44: Overall, manure and compost amendments generally increased SOC sequestration despite use of conventional tillage. The rate of SOC change decreased over time, however, organic amendments still had a net positive impact on sequestration even after several decades of application. This finding supports a dynamic equilibrium model where SOC pool equilibriums can be changed by management practices. Results also indicate that the negative SOC impacts of occasional tillage in no-till production systems could be counteracted by use of organic amendments or cover-cropping.

47: Renewable fuel programs are being used as the blueprint for future legislation. What is accepted by the California Air Resources Board is the de-facto standard which other states follow in absence of US federal legislation. In large part, legislation mandating bioproduct sustainability has been pathway focused. There is an ongoing shift toward ecolabeling of products. Unfortunately, soil health and the effects of crop production systems on individual crops/products are not frequently quantified as part of these assessments. The climate-smart commodity programs are beginning to correct this oversight. There has been a movement to develop a Midwest clean transportation standard. Within this standard and the management practices being evaluated as part of the climate-smart commodities programs, it is likely that soon soil health factors such as SOC or biodiversity will be included in quantitative analyses and policies.

Farmers want sustainable production. They want to pass their farms on to their children and grandchildren. If they are economically able, they will select practices that improve sustainability and enhance the productivity of their farms. Part of the challenge they face is understanding how their management practices affect their ability to get climate-smart commodity premiums. Most farmers plan their land use and management practices around yield. Communicating with them in terms of yield impacts and targets can help farmers select climate-smart practices and increase participation in climate- smart commodity programs.

49: Oilseed production life cycle analyses are sensitive to farm management. To meet feedstock standards, farm production will need to meet regional production and emission thresholds. As an emerging industrial feedstock alternative, Brassica carinata (carinata) farm production emissions thresholds need to be well understood to establish and scale regionalized production and guarantee feedstock marketability. This analysis estimated the life cycle impacts of carinata feedstock production due to differing regional management practices within South Dakota. Within the northern Grain Belt, conservative analyses show that carinata yields as low as 500-1000 kg/ha (10-20 bu/ac) can meet United States (US) feedstock life cycle emission thresholds if produced using low input and conservation management practices. Current regional no-till carinata production management practices and yields meet US emission thresholds.
50: <header>6.1. What are LCAs and why do farmers need them? </header>

There is a growing need to balance societal demands with resource limitations. Standardized environmental accounting systems, called life cycle assessments (LCAs) have been developed to improve sustainability-related decision making. Life cycle assessments are used to quantify the environmental performance of products, processes or services, and is increasingly being used as a basis for decision making by purchasers along the supply chain and consumers. LCA addresses the environmental aspects and potential environmental impacts such as the use of resources and the environmental consequences of releases throughout a product's life cycle from raw material acquisition through production, use, end-of-life treatment, recycling and final disposal (Figure 6.1).

These tools can and are applied to anything we, as humans, affect. The goal of an LCA is to better understand our resources use, identify weaknesses and strengths, and use them to improve efficiency and sustainability. Within renewable fuel and biofuel LCAs, agriculture is often a critical raw material source and significant contributor to life cycle impacts. As a result, the efficiency and sustainability of agricultural production affects all resulting products and processes.
Figure 6.1. Conceptual process diagram showing life cycle stages.
51: <header>6.1. What are LCAs and why do farmers need them? </header>

There is a growing need to balance societal demands with resource limitations. Standardized environmental accounting systems, called life cycle assessments (LCAs) have been developed to improve sustainability-related decision making. Life cycle assessments are used to quantify the environmental performance of products, processes or services, and is increasingly being used as a basis for decision making by purchasers along the supply chain and consumers. LCA addresses the environmental aspects and potential environmental impacts such as the use of resources and the environmental consequences of releases throughout a product's life cycle from raw material acquisition through production, use, end-of-life treatment, recycling and final disposal (Figure 6.1).

These tools can and are applied to anything we, as humans, affect. The goal of an LCA is to better understand our resources use, identify weaknesses and strengths, and use them to improve efficiency and sustainability. Within renewable fuel and biofuel LCAs, agriculture is often a critical raw material source and significant contributor to life cycle impacts. As a result, the efficiency and sustainability of agricultural production affects all resulting products and processes.
Figure 6.1. Conceptual process diagram showing life cycle stages.
52: Biofuel and renewable fuel production is being driven by industrial and governmental climate change and renewable energy policies. There are various national and regional mandates and rules and voluntary industrial commitments; these differing market requirements need to be considered when evaluating and using LCAs. Within the

United States, national biofuel and renewable fuel production targets are set annually as part of the Renewable Fuel Standard and was the focus of this life cycle impact analysis (LCIA). This federal program is used to incentivize investment and production of alternatives to liquid fossil fuels through legislatively mandated greenhouse gas (GHG) emission reduction targets and credits called renewable identification numbers (RINs).

These RINs are assigned based on federally approved conversion technologies and feedstocks based on life cycle GHG emission reductions and fuel category. Feedstock sourcing and production methods can be a significant contributor to fuel life cycle GHG emissions. As a biofuel and renewable fuel feedstock, at minimum, carinata farm production GHG emissions need to average below certain threshold to generate RINs. However, further reducing farm-level GHG emissions can increase feedstock value and marketability.
53: Decision-makers need to better understand these regional thresholds and how regionalized feedstock production can assist in meeting emission reduction and resiliency targets. To accomplish this for the northern Grain Belt, an extensive cradle-to-farm gate sensitivity analysis focusing on field-level farm management was conducted. This analysis was designed to address the two primary controls on regional productivity, moisture and fertility. Our goal was to determine the minimum carinata yields South Dakota farmers would need to meet GHG emission thresholds and to create an easy-to- use lookup table for producers.
54: This analysis was conducted using data from South Dakota. South Dakota is one of the United States' most rural and least industrialized areas and is located within the northern part of the North American Grain Belt and Great Plains (Figure 6.2). South Dakota contains multiple agroecological, geological, and climatic transitions with an east-west gradient of wet to dry and north-south gradient from cold to warm. The western part of South Dakota is semi-arid and dominated by wheat-fallow crop and rangeland beef cow-calf production. In this area, one grain crop is traditionally produced every other year. To the least, the State's climate is sub-humid and contains highly productive annual corn-soybean cropping systems with diverse livestock production systems including beef, dairy, poultry, and swine. Within the northern half of the state, it is often too cold to reliably produce dormant-seeded crops, such as winter wheat, which are present in southern areas. Many croplands to the far eastern and western parts of South Dakota still use conventional tillage, whereas some counties within the central climatic transition areas have 100% no till adoption and were early adopters of this and many other conservation management practices. Nearly all croplands within South Dakota are non-irrigated and are dependent on the region's inconsistent precipitation due to lack of surface and ground water availability. As a transitional area from pine-forested mountains and short-grass steppes to tall-grass prairies and glacial lakes and wetlands, analytical results from South Dakota are applicable to a wide range of agroecological systems within the Great Plains region of North America.
