Goal:|Determine attributional LCA sensitivity to yield, tillage management, and fertilizer use Scope:|Farm production (cradle-to-field gate), Diesel-type renewable fuel combustion (cradle-to-grave) Functional Unit:|kg carinata, MJ renewable fuel Allocation:|Mass, Energy with and without soymeal substitution Inventory:|SDSU, NDSU, and USDA-ARS field observations aggregated following Agri-footprint 4.0 methods (IPCC and usetox default emissions) supplemented with US LCI and EcoInvent v.3 libraries Impact Analysis:|ReCiPe midpoint world hierarchist 1.12 Software:|SimaPro 8.2 Uncertainty:|Pedigree analysis within a Monte Carlo (n=1000) Sensitivity:|Field inputs with resulting products and emissions matrix of 288 farm gate scenarios
61: To establish the maximum farm gate GHG emission threshold, a full cradle-to- grave from feedstock production to renewable fuel combustion was considered. This system boundary was used only to establish farm gate maximum GHG emission levels, the focus of this analysis was on farm gate feedstock production life cycle contributions. A matrix of regional agronomic management practices, inputs, and field emissions within a cradle-to-farm gate system boundary to establish regional feedstock field management was evaluated. Inputs and emissions were mass allocated with respect to their contribution towards production of a kg of carinata based on 500 kg/ha yield increments. Primary inputs included: seed, direct land, rainwater, diesel, pesticide, and fertilizer used. Primary emissions included default agricultural emissions based on IPCC (2006) estimates for fertilizer-sourced ammonia and fossil CO2 as well as direct and indirect N2O and NO3- from residues and fertilizers. Soil organic carbon sequestration was not included in this analysis. Pesticide emission factors following Agri-footprint 4.0 inventory methods (as derived from Usetox) to soil, air, and water were estimated based on active ingredients and regional residence time.
62: Based on back-calculations from a D5 category RIN renewable fuel cradle-to- grave analysis (advanced biofuel with >50% GHG emission reductions for diesel-type fuels with a fossil fuel baseline of 91.8 g CO2eq/MJ) of regionalized production (transport distance <1000 km), feedstock producers would need to have carinata production impacts less than 1.0 kg CO2eq/kg carinata if soybean meal (soymeal)

substitution was used in the analysis. Replacement of high demand food products with multiple demands with industrial, alternative products is an accepted analytical method that is commonly used. Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

Within South Dakota, there are currently limited renewable fuel or biofuel resources available. As a result, the impacts of renewable fuel use in farm production cannot be currently quantified without substantially increasing the uncertainty of these estimates. However, preliminary analyses indicate that if fully renewable fuels (e.g. B100) were used, the yield targets could be reduced by approximately 300 kg/ha (-5 bu/ac) for no till management and by ~600 kg/ha (-10 0 bu/ac) for conventional tillage. Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.

Figure 6.3. Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

Figure 6.5. Carinata yield matrices showing approximate, minimum average yields needed to ensure that feedstock field gate RIN GHG emission reduction thresholds are met both (A) with and (B) without soybean meal substitution using Système International (SI) units.

Within South Dakota, there are currently limited renewable fuel or biofuel resources available. As a result, the impacts of renewable fuel use in farm production cannot be currently quantified without substantially increasing the uncertainty of these estimates. However, preliminary analyses indicate that if fully renewable fuels (e.g. B100) were used, the yield targets could be reduced by approximately 300 kg/ha (-5 bu/ac) for no till management and by ~600 kg/ha (-10 0 bu/ac) for conventional tillage. Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.
64: Based on back-calculations from a D5 category RIN renewable fuel cradle-to- grave analysis (advanced biofuel with >50% GHG emission reductions for diesel-type fuels with a fossil fuel baseline of 91.8 g CO2eq/MJ) of regionalized production (transport distance <1000 km), feedstock producers would need to have carinata production impacts less than 1.0 kg CO2eq/kg carinata if soybean meal (soymeal)

substitution was used in the analysis. Replacement of high demand food products with multiple demands with industrial, alternative products is an accepted analytical method that is commonly used. Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Figure 6.3. Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

Figure 6.5. Carinata yield matrices showing approximate, minimum average yields needed to ensure that feedstock field gate RIN GHG emission reduction thresholds are met both (A) with and (B) without soybean meal substitution using Système International (SI) units.

Within South Dakota, there are currently limited renewable fuel or biofuel resources available. As a result, the impacts of renewable fuel use in farm production cannot be currently quantified without substantially increasing the uncertainty of these estimates. However, preliminary analyses indicate that if fully renewable fuels (e.g. B100) were used, the yield targets could be reduced by approximately 300 kg/ha (-5 bu/ac) for no till management and by ~600 kg/ha (-10 0 bu/ac) for conventional tillage. Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.
67: Based on back-calculations from a D5 category RIN renewable fuel cradle-to- grave analysis (advanced biofuel with >50% GHG emission reductions for diesel-type fuels with a fossil fuel baseline of 91.8 g CO2eq/MJ) of regionalized production (transport distance <1000 km), feedstock producers would need to have carinata production impacts less than 1.0 kg CO2eq/kg carinata if soybean meal (soymeal)

substitution was used in the analysis. Replacement of high demand food products with multiple demands with industrial, alternative products is an accepted analytical method that is commonly used. Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Figure 6.3. Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

Figure 6.5. Carinata yield matrices showing approximate, minimum average yields needed to ensure that feedstock field gate RIN GHG emission reduction thresholds are met both (A) with and (B) without soybean meal substitution using Système International (SI) units.

Within South Dakota, there are currently limited renewable fuel or biofuel resources available. As a result, the impacts of renewable fuel use in farm production cannot be currently quantified without substantially increasing the uncertainty of these estimates. However, preliminary analyses indicate that if fully renewable fuels (e.g. B100) were used, the yield targets could be reduced by approximately 300 kg/ha (-5 bu/ac) for no till management and by ~600 kg/ha (-10 0 bu/ac) for conventional tillage. Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.
70: Based on back-calculations from a D5 category RIN renewable fuel cradle-to- grave analysis (advanced biofuel with >50% GHG emission reductions for diesel-type fuels with a fossil fuel baseline of 91.8 g CO2eq/MJ) of regionalized production (transport distance <1000 km), feedstock producers would need to have carinata production impacts less than 1.0 kg CO2eq/kg carinata if soybean meal (soymeal)

substitution was used in the analysis. Replacement of high demand food products with multiple demands with industrial, alternative products is an accepted analytical method that is commonly used. Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Figure 6.3. Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

Figure 6.5. Carinata yield matrices showing approximate, minimum average yields needed to ensure that feedstock field gate RIN GHG emission reduction thresholds are met both (A) with and (B) without soybean meal substitution using Système International (SI) units.

Within South Dakota, there are currently limited renewable fuel or biofuel resources available. As a result, the impacts of renewable fuel use in farm production cannot be currently quantified without substantially increasing the uncertainty of these estimates. However, preliminary analyses indicate that if fully renewable fuels (e.g. B100) were used, the yield targets could be reduced by approximately 300 kg/ha (-5 bu/ac) for no till management and by ~600 kg/ha (-10 0 bu/ac) for conventional tillage. Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.

Total number of pesticide applications and types did not substantially affect the GHG-

based yield threshold results. However, increased agrochemical use negatively affected water resources and increased aquatic ecotoxicity and eutrophication impacts of production. These analytical results were constructed for South Dakota dryland production crop production areas. However, the conservative structure of the analysis should address many regional variances in equipment and management. Activities which substantially increase field inputs, such as heavy use of irrigation, would require additional analyses. Past long-term no till field trials within South Dakota have yielded ~2000 kg/ha without any fertilizer inputs, SO projected yields are feasible.
75: Based on back-calculations from a D5 category RIN renewable fuel cradle-to- grave analysis (advanced biofuel with >50% GHG emission reductions for diesel-type fuels with a fossil fuel baseline of 91.8 g CO2eq/MJ) of regionalized production (transport distance <1000 km), feedstock producers would need to have carinata production impacts less than 1.0 kg CO2eq/kg carinata if soybean meal (soymeal)

substitution was used in the analysis. Replacement of high demand food products with multiple demands with industrial, alternative products is an accepted analytical method that is commonly used. Without soymeal substitution, carinata farm-level production impacts would need to be less than 0.7 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. The coefficient of variance maximum within climate change impact uncertainty analyses for simulations was just under 10%. This statistical value was used because it provided the most conservative uncertainty estimate and was used to make results more broadly applicable. This rough factor of safety was used to reduce average field gate, life cycle GHG emission thresholds to 0.9 and 0.6 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with and without soymeal substitution, respectively (Figure 6.3). This reduction should account for expected variances in transport distance, storage, and supply chains.

Figure 6.3. Box plot diagrams showing the range of northern Great Plains field gate life cycle GHG emission estimates generated by all simulated fertilization alternatives using (a) fuel use based on 2018 MN Extension equipment estimates and (b) extension estimates plus EcoInvent field activity estimates to simulate variations in equipment age. Lower green line represents a conservative estimate of maximum field gate emissions with no meal substitution to generate RINs and the upper red line represents maximum field gate emissions if soybean meal substitution is used.

The matrix of simulations developed by combining the listed model components and expected yields were modified to estimate variances in farm equipment age and fuel use efficiency. Fuel use varies substantially due to equipment size, type, and age. Most farms within South Dakota use a mixture of old and new as well as large and small equipment that is difficult to characterize and accurately represent. Original simulations used regionally-specific, mid-sized equipment fuel use survey results and the modified simulations use this plus average EcoInvent field activity-specific inventories (Figure 6.3). This more or less doubled the fuel use to provide a conservative maximum emissions due to expected variability in equipment age, type, and size and other general energy use consumptions.

Use of this fuel use modification increased estimated field gate GHG impacts on average by 24%, 41% and 233% with high, low, and no fertilizer use respectively. Fuel use was the primary factor which differentiated results between tillage practices. No-till production used less fuel than conventional tillage. Fertilizer type affected field gate emissions by approximately 20% with high fertilization and 17% with moderate fertilization. Use of ammonium sulfate reduced fertilization effects while ammonium nitrate increased GHG emissions over urea. The differences in the fertilization types results were reflective of life cycle production differences. This analysis did not account for uptake efficiency or application differences. Within the study region, urea is typically used because it is most readily available but does not necessarily have the best N use efficiency due to field conditions, application method and timing, and climatic variations.
A matrix of emissions was created for each scenario and compiled to identify the minimum yield needed to meet GHG feedstock emission thresholds (Figures 6.4 and 6.5).

Use of meal substitution resulted in 30% lower yield thresholds on average. Uncertainty analyses indicate that climate change emissions are likely to vary by up to +0.06 kg CO2eq/kg carinata. Due to the intentionally conservative structure of the analyses and assumptions, minor differences in model parameterization or inputs (e.g. one more or less tillage pass, spraying event, or slightly more or less fertilizer) are unlikely to alter yield threshold results.

77: The European Union has more stringent emission reduction targets for biofuels. Based on a 60% emission reduction from a fossil fuel baseline of 83.8 g CO2eq/MJ, the approximate field gate life cycle emission thresholds for feedstock production would be 0.75 kg CO2eq/kg carinata with soymeal substitution. Due to the highly conservative analysis construction, this means that the chart shown in Figure 6.5b would safely exceed the yield thresholds needed to market carinata internationally if soymeal substitution is used in the LCA calculations. These analyses did not include soil carbon sequestration. Policy pertaining to the use of soil carbon and methodology of calculation within renewable fuel and biofuel emission reduction calculations is variable. Use of soil carbon in these estimates would decrease yield targets.
78: These results represent the possible range of life cycle GHG impacts due to northern Grain Belt carinata production, however these results do not address existing regional field conditions, management practice duration, climatic differences, or likelihood of occurrence. Production of industrial feedstocks, such as carinata, are being targeted within marginal land areas or during fallow. Marginal lands and fallow replacement feedstock production can be expected to produce lower yields, have increased crop failures, and likely require additional resources and effort - all of which will likely increase field-level GHG emissions impact and risk of production. Crops produced within these areas will be expected to not only maintain soil health and productivity, but also improve future crop productivity and help to rebuild soils. Marginal lands and fallow replacements are likely to be a key component of food, fuel, fiber, and feed production strategies across most geographies and future markets. Incentives to encourage use of marginal land areas or off-season production are needed to systemically improve agronomic production capacities.
79: Synthetic fertilizer use substantially effects field gate GHG emissions. Recently revised regional fertility guidelines for North Dakota have new, differing climatic- and tillage-based recommendations. South Dakota is in the process of revising statewide fertility guidelines and will likely soon release updated recommendations. The revised North Dakota guidelines demonstrate that regenerative and conservation farm management practices work and have multiple benefits. But, they can take many years of

time, resources, and effort to reap their rewards. The duration of no till within cold, temperate climates significantly affects fertilizer requirements. After adoption of no till within the Dakotas, five to ten years of dedicated effort can be expected before long-term benefits such as increased fertility occur. In the short-term, fields recently converted to no till will likely require additional inputs and management effort but will likely increase long-term productivity. Semi-arid and colder areas have been shown to retain fertility better than sub-humid and warmer climatic areas. A critical component of current Grain Belt crop production is use of soil fertility testing.

Soil tests results over the last decade have shown and new fertility guidelines estimate most cropped fields already within the Dakotas contain an average of 45 kg N/ha. If carinata is planted after a legume cover crop or crop, an additional 45 kg N/ha or more is likely available in the soil. In sub-humid areas, approximately 20 kg N/ha more is available if fields are under long-term no till but can be reduced by approximately the same amount if no till has been recently adopted. In semi-arid areas the benefit is greater, with almost 40 kg N/ha more on average available within long-term no till fields. Recent on-going studies indicate that long-term winter livestock grazing of croplands can provide between 50 to 90 kg N/ha depending on crop rotation differences and variations in grazing frequency, intensity, and duration. Within the Dakotas, rotations are a necessity due to climatic limitations and it is not unusual to have one of more sacrificial crops to support the primary cash crop (e.g. wheat, corn, or sunflowers) within the rotation. As a result of good field and soil management, over 100 kg N/ha can be available without use of synthetic fertilizers if rotations are structured with carinata as a primary crop. On average, carinata can be regionally produced within the northern Grain

Belt with only moderate synthetic fertilization requirements if appropriate rotational management is used and if it is a profitable cash crop for farmers. Oilseed production more often than not, will still require some moderate fertilizer use (Figure 6.6). But, the quantity of fertilized used can be significantly reduced if regenerative management practices are adopted, thereby reducing the yield needed to meet footprint thresholds. It is important to note that use of non-composted organic amendments such as manure do contribute to field-level GHG emissions, especially if directly deposited on fields. However, these emissions are shared with livestock co-products and are typically less than that of synthetic fertilizers.

Figure 6.6. Field gate life cycle GHG emissions estimate a carinata yield of 2000 kg/ha produced using urea as a fertilizer with adjustments for management practice-based fertilization guidelines. Scenario urea use ranged from 112 to 12 kg N/ha (100 to 10 lb N/ac) from left to right.

As a result of good field and soil management, over 100 kg N/ha can be available without use of synthetic fertilizers if rotations are structured with carinata as a primary crop. On average, carinata can be regionally produced within the northern Grain

Belt with only moderate synthetic fertilization requirements if appropriate rotational management is used and if it is a profitable cash crop for farmers. Oilseed production more often than not, will still require some moderate fertilizer use (Figure 6.6). But, the quantity of fertilized used can be significantly reduced if regenerative management practices are adopted, thereby reducing the yield needed to meet footprint thresholds. It is important to note that use of non-composted organic amendments such as manure do contribute to field-level GHG emissions, especially if directly deposited on fields. However, these emissions are shared with livestock co-products and are typically less than that of synthetic fertilizers.

Figure 6.6. Field gate life cycle GHG emissions estimate a carinata yield of 2000 kg/ha produced using urea as a fertilizer with adjustments for management practice-based fertilization guidelines. Scenario urea use ranged from 112 to 12 kg N/ha (100 to 10 lb N/ac) from left to right.
82: Synthetic fertilizer use substantially effects field gate GHG emissions. Recently revised regional fertility guidelines for North Dakota have new, differing climatic- and tillage-based recommendations. South Dakota is in the process of revising statewide fertility guidelines and will likely soon release updated recommendations. The revised North Dakota guidelines demonstrate that regenerative and conservation farm management practices work and have multiple benefits. But, they can take many years of

time, resources, and effort to reap their rewards. The duration of no till within cold, temperate climates significantly affects fertilizer requirements. After adoption of no till within the Dakotas, five to ten years of dedicated effort can be expected before long-term benefits such as increased fertility occur. In the short-term, fields recently converted to no till will likely require additional inputs and management effort but will likely increase long-term productivity. Semi-arid and colder areas have been shown to retain fertility better than sub-humid and warmer climatic areas. A critical component of current Grain Belt crop production is use of soil fertility testing.

Soil tests results over the last decade have shown and new fertility guidelines estimate most cropped fields already within the Dakotas contain an average of 45 kg N/ha. If carinata is planted after a legume cover crop or crop, an additional 45 kg N/ha or more is likely available in the soil. In sub-humid areas, approximately 20 kg N/ha more is available if fields are under long-term no till but can be reduced by approximately the same amount if no till has been recently adopted. In semi-arid areas the benefit is greater, with almost 40 kg N/ha more on average available within long-term no till fields. Recent on-going studies indicate that long-term winter livestock grazing of croplands can provide between 50 to 90 kg N/ha depending on crop rotation differences and variations in grazing frequency, intensity, and duration. Within the Dakotas, rotations are a necessity due to climatic limitations and it is not unusual to have one of more sacrificial crops to support the primary cash crop (e.g. wheat, corn, or sunflowers) within the rotation. As a result of good field and soil management, over 100 kg N/ha can be available without use of synthetic fertilizers if rotations are structured with carinata as a primary crop. On average, carinata can be regionally produced within the northern Grain

Belt with only moderate synthetic fertilization requirements if appropriate rotational management is used and if it is a profitable cash crop for farmers. Oilseed production more often than not, will still require some moderate fertilizer use (Figure 6.6). But, the quantity of fertilized used can be significantly reduced if regenerative management practices are adopted, thereby reducing the yield needed to meet footprint thresholds. It is important to note that use of non-composted organic amendments such as manure do contribute to field-level GHG emissions, especially if directly deposited on fields. However, these emissions are shared with livestock co-products and are typically less than that of synthetic fertilizers.

Figure 6.6. Field gate life cycle GHG emissions estimate a carinata yield of 2000 kg/ha produced using urea as a fertilizer with adjustments for management practice-based fertilization guidelines. Scenario urea use ranged from 112 to 12 kg N/ha (100 to 10 lb N/ac) from left to right.
83: <header>6.12. Importance of Agroecosystem Health to GHG Field Gate Emissions </header>
Healthy soils and agroecosystems can reduce agrochemical inputs and improve long-term productivity. Within the Dakotas regenerative farming is a minimalist,

adaptive no till management approach which selectively uses organic farming techniques to create beneficial biofeedback and synergies but still uses agrochemicals if needed to maintain productivity. A key component of regenerative management is biodiversity, which is something that single impact analyses like GHG footprints or even comprehensive LCAs do not characterize well. Widespread and frequent application of insecticides, fungicides, and herbicides impairs biodiversity and ultimately results in loss of ecosystem services, particularly pollination services. Management practices which support insect pollinators can serve to stabilize or boost oilseed yields. Bee hives within or near oilseed fields can serve to increase pollination services while producing an economically and environmentally important co-product. Activities such as winter residue or cover crop grazing can support soil development and fertility. Producer adoption of crop-livestock integration and other regenerative farm management practices is increasing within the Dakotas. Recent 2018 South Dakota State University (SDSU) commodity crop producer surveys indicate that almost half of surveyed producers are now using cover crops on at least one of their fields and over half are now using integrated crop-livestock production. This survey also indicates that almost all the respondents use soil testing to manage fertilizer applications (Schad, 2018 personal communication). Many agricultural producers in South Dakota and the greater Grain Belt region are actively working to improve agroecosystem health because it has long- term economic benefits. A key tenant of regenerative farming is diversification. Production of carinata and other industrial oilseeds can help to diversify regional farm production and improve agroecosystem health.
Long-term regenerative, no till farm management within the northern Grain Belt

of North America reduces life cycle impacts to the extent that national and international export of regionally feedstocks produced is feasible, even within stringent field gate GHG emission thresholds.

88: Ecolabeling will increase consumer awareness of the impact their consumption and choices have. Farmers will need to adapt and adopt more sustainable management practices to maintain or increase their share of the market. If done with care and consideration, positive cycles and systems rewarding improvements in efficiency and sustainability can be developed. Evaluations and tools such as those presented in this dissertation can assist in this transition.
